kids.question: What did God create on the very first day?
kids.explanation: God said "Let there be light" and there was light (Genesis 1:3).

kids.question: What did God make on Day 6?
kids.explanation: God made land animals and man on the sixth day (Genesis 1:24-27).

kids.question: How many days did it take God to create everything?
kids.explanation: God created everything in six days and rested on the seventh (Genesis 1:31–2:2).

teens.question: What did God create on the second day?
teens.explanation: God made the firmament and divided the waters above from the waters beneath (Genesis 1:6-8).

teens.question: On which day did God create the sun, moon, and stars?
teens.explanation: God made two great lights and the stars on the fourth day (Genesis 1:14-19).

teens.question: What did God say about everything He had made at the end of Day 6?
teens.explanation: God saw every thing that he had made, and behold, it was very good (Genesis 1:31).

teens.question: In whose image was man created?
teens.explanation: God said, "Let us make man in our image, after our likeness" (Genesis 1:26).

teens.question: What was man given dominion over?
teens.option_b: The fish, birds, and every living thing
teens.explanation: God gave man dominion over the fish, fowl, cattle, and every creeping thing (Genesis 1:26, 28).

adults.question: What phrase is used in Genesis 1:2 to describe the state of the earth before creation?
adults.option_b: Empty and barren
adults.explanation: The earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep (Genesis 1:2).

adults.question: What moved upon the face of the waters in Genesis 1:2?
adults.option_b: The Spirit of God
adults.explanation: And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters (Genesis 1:2).

adults.question: What did God call the firmament?
adults.option_b: Heaven
adults.explanation: And God called the firmament Heaven (Genesis 1:8).

adults.question: On which day were seed-bearing herbs and fruit trees created?
adults.option_b: Day 3
adults.explanation: On the third day God said, "Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree" (Genesis 1:11-13).

adults.question: What two purposes did God assign to the lights in the firmament?
adults.option_b: To divide the day from the night and for signs, seasons, days, and years
adults.option_c: To shine upon the earth and to guide travelers
adults.option_d: To rule the angels and to mark holy days
adults.explanation: God said let them be for signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years, and to divide the day from the night (Genesis 1:14).

adults.question: What Hebrew word is translated "God" throughout Genesis 1?
adults.option_b: Adonai
adults.explanation: The Hebrew word used for God in Genesis 1 is Elohim, a plural noun conveying majesty.

adults.question: What specific blessing did God give to the sea creatures and birds on Day 5?
adults.option_b: Be fruitful, and multiply
adults.explanation: And God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply (Genesis 1:22).

adults.question: What does Genesis 1:26 reveal by using the phrase "Let us make man in our image"?
adults.option_a: God was speaking to the animals
adults.option_b: A plurality within the Godhead
adults.explanation: The plural "us" and "our" in Genesis 1:26 has traditionally been understood as reflecting the Trinity or a divine plurality.

kids.question: What did God do on the seventh day?
kids.explanation: God rested on the seventh day from all his work (Genesis 2:2).

kids.question: What was the name of the garden God made for Adam?
kids.explanation: God planted a garden eastward in Eden (Genesis 2:8).

kids.question: How did God make Eve?
kids.explanation: God took one of Adam's ribs and made a woman (Genesis 2:21-22).

teens.question: What job did God give Adam in the garden?
teens.option_b: To dress it and keep it
teens.option_c: To rule over all the nations
teens.explanation: God put Adam in the garden to dress it and to keep it (Genesis 2:15).

teens.question: Which tree were Adam and Eve forbidden to eat from?
teens.option_b: The tree of knowledge of good and evil
teens.explanation: God commanded not to eat of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil (Genesis 2:17).

teens.question: What did God say would happen if Adam ate from the forbidden tree?
teens.explanation: God said, "In the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die" (Genesis 2:17).

teens.question: Why did God decide to make Eve?
teens.option_b: God said it was not good for man to be alone
teens.explanation: God said, "It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him an help meet" (Genesis 2:18).

teens.question: What task did Adam perform that showed his authority over the animals?
teens.explanation: Adam gave names to all cattle, fowl, and every beast of the field (Genesis 2:20).

adults.question: From what material did God form man?
adults.option_b: Dust of the ground
adults.explanation: The LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground (Genesis 2:7).

adults.question: What did God breathe into man's nostrils?
adults.option_b: Life and knowledge
adults.explanation: God breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul (Genesis 2:7).

adults.question: How many rivers branched from the river that went out of Eden?
adults.option_b: Three
adults.explanation: A river went out of Eden and parted into four heads (Genesis 2:10).

adults.question: Which of these is one of the four rivers named in Genesis 2?
adults.option_b: Nile
adults.explanation: The four rivers are Pison, Gihon, Hiddekel (Tigris), and Euphrates (Genesis 2:11-14).

adults.question: What was the name of the land associated with gold and bdellium?
adults.option_b: Havilah
adults.explanation: The land of Havilah, where there is gold, and bdellium and the onyx stone (Genesis 2:11-12).

adults.question: What foundational statement about marriage appears in Genesis 2:24?
adults.option_a: A man shall honor his father and mother
adults.option_b: A man shall leave his father and mother and cleave unto his wife
adults.option_c: A man shall take a wife from his own people
adults.option_d: A man and his wife shall multiply and fill the earth
adults.explanation: Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh (Genesis 2:24).

adults.question: What was the condition of Adam and Eve described in the last verse of chapter 2?
adults.option_b: They were naked and not ashamed
adults.explanation: They were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed (Genesis 2:25).

adults.question: What significant theological truth is established by Adam finding no suitable helper among the animals?
adults.option_b: Man's unique nature requires a companion of equal dignity
adults.option_c: God made a mistake in creating animals first
adults.option_d: Adam was not satisfied with God's creation
adults.explanation: No suitable helper was found among the animals, showing that man needed a companion of his own kind, made in God's image (Genesis 2:20).

kids.question: Which animal tricked Eve in the garden?
kids.explanation: The serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field and spoke to Eve (Genesis 3:1).

kids.question: What did Adam and Eve do after they ate the forbidden fruit?
kids.option_a: They ran away from the garden
kids.explanation: Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God (Genesis 3:8).

kids.question: What did God use to make clothes for Adam and Eve?
kids.explanation: God made coats of skins and clothed them (Genesis 3:21).

teens.question: What lie did the serpent tell Eve about eating the fruit?
teens.option_c: You will turn into an animal
teens.explanation: The serpent said, "Ye shall not surely die" (Genesis 3:4).

teens.question: What were the three things Eve saw about the forbidden tree that tempted her?
teens.option_a: It was tall, green, and beautiful
teens.option_b: It was good for food, pleasant to the eyes, and desired to make one wise
teens.option_c: It was sweet, large, and colorful
teens.option_d: It was near, ripe, and fragrant
teens.explanation: The woman saw that the tree was good for food, pleasant to the eyes, and desired to make one wise (Genesis 3:6).

teens.question: What did Adam and Eve make to cover themselves?
teens.explanation: They sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons (Genesis 3:7).

teens.question: What was part of the curse on the woman?
teens.option_b: She would have sorrow in childbearing
teens.option_c: She could never return to Eden
teens.explanation: God said, "I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children" (Genesis 3:16).

teens.question: What was placed at the east of the garden of Eden to guard the tree of life?
teens.explanation: God placed Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life (Genesis 3:24).

adults.question: What is the serpent described as in Genesis 3:1?
adults.option_b: More subtil than any beast of the field
adults.option_d: The oldest animal in the garden
adults.explanation: Now the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field which the LORD God had made (Genesis 3:1).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Genesis 3:15, often called the protoevangelium?
adults.option_b: It is the first Messianic prophecy, foretelling the seed of the woman crushing the serpent
adults.option_d: It promises the land of Canaan
adults.explanation: Genesis 3:15 is the first Messianic prophecy: "her seed...shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel."

adults.question: What specific curse was placed upon the ground because of Adam?
adults.option_b: It would be cursed and bring forth thorns and thistles
adults.explanation: Cursed is the ground for thy sake; thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth (Genesis 3:17-18).

adults.question: What does the phrase "the eyes of them both were opened" in Genesis 3:7 refer to?
adults.option_b: They became aware of their nakedness and experienced moral awareness
adults.explanation: Their eyes were opened and they knew they were naked — they gained moral awareness of sin (Genesis 3:7).

adults.question: What reason does God give in Genesis 3:22 for expelling man from the garden?
adults.option_a: Man was too sinful to remain
adults.option_b: Lest man eat of the tree of life and live forever in a fallen state
adults.option_c: The garden was no longer suitable
adults.option_d: Man needed to learn to farm
adults.explanation: Lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever (Genesis 3:22).

adults.question: What name did Adam give his wife, and what does it mean?
adults.option_b: Eve, because she was the mother of all living
adults.explanation: Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living (Genesis 3:20).

adults.question: How did Adam respond when God confronted him about eating the fruit?
adults.option_b: He blamed the woman God gave him
adults.explanation: Adam said, "The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat" (Genesis 3:12).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of God making coats of skins for Adam and Eve?
adults.option_a: God wanted them to be warm
adults.option_b: It foreshadows substitutionary atonement — an animal died to cover their sin
adults.option_c: God was teaching them to sew
adults.option_d: It was a punishment for their sin
adults.explanation: God's provision of animal skins required the death of an animal, foreshadowing the principle of blood atonement (Genesis 3:21).

kids.question: Who were Adam and Eve's first two sons?
kids.explanation: Eve bare Cain, and again bare his brother Abel (Genesis 4:1-2).

kids.question: What was Abel's job?
kids.explanation: Abel was a keeper of sheep (Genesis 4:2).

kids.question: What terrible thing did Cain do to Abel?
kids.explanation: Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him (Genesis 4:8).

teens.question: Why was God not pleased with Cain's offering?
teens.option_b: God did not have respect unto Cain and his offering, while Abel brought the firstlings of his flock
teens.explanation: The Lord had respect unto Abel and his offering, but unto Cain and his offering he had not respect (Genesis 4:4-5).

teens.question: What did God warn Cain about before the murder?
teens.option_b: That sin lieth at the door and desires to have him
teens.option_d: That he would be cursed to wander
teens.explanation: God said, "Sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over it" (Genesis 4:7).

teens.question: What was Cain's punishment for killing Abel?
teens.option_b: He was cursed from the earth and became a fugitive and vagabond
teens.option_d: He lost all his crops forever
teens.explanation: God said Cain would be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth (Genesis 4:12).

teens.question: What did God put on Cain so no one would kill him?
teens.explanation: The LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him (Genesis 4:15).

teens.question: Where did Cain go to live after leaving God's presence?
teens.explanation: Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden (Genesis 4:16).

adults.question: What offering did Abel bring to the Lord?
adults.option_b: The firstlings of his flock and the fat thereof
adults.explanation: Abel brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof (Genesis 4:4).

adults.question: What famous question did Cain ask God in Genesis 4:9?
adults.option_b: Where is my brother?
adults.explanation: Cain said, "Am I my brother's keeper?" (Genesis 4:9).

adults.question: What does God say Abel's blood is doing in Genesis 4:10?
adults.option_b: Crying unto God from the ground
adults.explanation: The voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground (Genesis 4:10).

adults.question: What city did Cain build, and after whom was it named?
adults.option_b: Enoch, after his son
adults.explanation: Cain builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch (Genesis 4:17).

adults.question: Who was Lamech in Cain's line, and what is he notable for?
adults.option_b: He took two wives and boasted of killing a man
adults.option_c: He built the tower of Babel
adults.option_d: He was the first to offer sacrifice
adults.explanation: Lamech took two wives and said he had slain a man to his wounding (Genesis 4:19, 23).

adults.question: Who was the father of all who handle the harp and organ?
adults.option_b: Jubal
adults.explanation: Jubal was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ (Genesis 4:21).

adults.question: Who was Tubal-cain, and what was he known for?
adults.option_b: An instructor of every artificer in brass and iron
adults.explanation: Tubal-cain was an instructor of every artificer in brass and iron (Genesis 4:22).

adults.question: What son was born to Adam and Eve to replace Abel?
adults.option_b: Jared
adults.explanation: Eve bare a son and called his name Seth, saying God hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel (Genesis 4:25).

kids.question: Who lived the longest in the Bible at 969 years?
kids.explanation: Methuselah lived nine hundred sixty and nine years (Genesis 5:27).

kids.question: What happened to Enoch instead of dying?
kids.explanation: Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him (Genesis 5:24).

kids.question: Who was Noah's father?
kids.explanation: Lamech begat a son and called his name Noah (Genesis 5:28-29).

teens.question: What unique phrase is used to describe Enoch's relationship with God?
teens.explanation: Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him (Genesis 5:24).

teens.question: How old was Adam when he died?
teens.explanation: All the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years: and he died (Genesis 5:5).

teens.question: What phrase is repeated throughout Genesis 5 about each patriarch?
teens.explanation: The recurring phrase "and he died" emphasizes the reality of death after the fall.

teens.question: What does Lamech say Noah's name means or relates to?
teens.option_a: Rest and comfort from their work
teens.explanation: Lamech called him Noah, saying, "This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil" (Genesis 5:29).

teens.question: How many sons of Noah are named in chapter 5?
teens.explanation: Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth — three sons (Genesis 5:32).

adults.question: In what likeness did God make Adam according to Genesis 5:1?
adults.option_a: In the likeness of the angels
adults.option_b: In the likeness of God
adults.explanation: In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God made he him (Genesis 5:1).

adults.question: In whose likeness did Adam beget Seth?
adults.option_b: In his own likeness, after his image
adults.explanation: Adam begat a son in his own likeness, after his image, and called his name Seth (Genesis 5:3).

adults.question: How old was Enoch when God took him?
adults.option_b: Three hundred and sixty-five years
adults.explanation: All the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five years (Genesis 5:23).

adults.question: What is notable about Enoch in the genealogy compared to every other patriarch?
adults.option_b: He lived the shortest and never died
adults.explanation: Unlike every other patriarch who "died," Enoch "was not; for God took him" (Genesis 5:24).

adults.question: How old was Noah when his three sons were born (approximately)?
adults.option_b: Three hundred years old
adults.explanation: Noah was five hundred years old: and Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth (Genesis 5:32).

adults.question: The genealogy in Genesis 5 traces the line from Adam to Noah through which son of Adam?
adults.option_b: Abel
adults.explanation: The godly line is traced through Seth, not Cain (Genesis 5:3-32).

adults.question: What theological purpose does the Genesis 5 genealogy serve?
adults.option_a: It shows that the earth is very old
adults.option_b: It establishes the unbroken line from Adam to Noah and the reality of death
adults.option_c: It proves that angels married humans
adults.option_d: It records all the people who ever lived
adults.explanation: The genealogy traces the covenant line from Adam to Noah, with "and he died" underscoring the consequence of the fall.

adults.question: How old was Methuselah when he died, and what is significant about the timing?
adults.option_a: He died at 900, before the flood
adults.option_b: He died at 969, the year of the flood
adults.option_c: He died at 950, after the flood
adults.option_d: He died at 777, a number of perfection
adults.explanation: Methuselah died at 969 years old. Calculating from the genealogy, his death occurred in the year of the flood (Genesis 5:25-27).

kids.question: Who did God ask to build a big boat?
kids.explanation: God told Noah to build an ark (Genesis 6:13-14).

kids.question: Why did God want to send a flood?
kids.option_a: Because it hadn't rained in a long time
kids.option_d: Because He wanted to fill the rivers
kids.explanation: God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth (Genesis 6:5).

kids.question: What was the big boat called?
kids.explanation: God told Noah to make an ark of gopher wood (Genesis 6:14).

teens.question: What does Genesis 6:5 say about the state of mankind?
teens.option_b: Every imagination of man's heart was only evil continually
teens.option_c: Most people were wicked but some were good
teens.explanation: Every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually (Genesis 6:5).

teens.question: What does it mean that Noah "found grace in the eyes of the LORD"?
teens.option_a: Noah earned God's favor by his works
teens.option_b: God chose to show unmerited favor to Noah
teens.option_c: Noah discovered a secret about God
teens.option_d: Noah prayed more than anyone else
teens.explanation: Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD — the first mention of grace in the Bible (Genesis 6:8).

teens.question: What kind of wood was the ark made from?
teens.explanation: God said, "Make thee an ark of gopher wood" (Genesis 6:14).

teens.question: How many of each kind of animal was Noah told to bring on the ark?
teens.explanation: Noah was to bring two of every sort into the ark, male and female (Genesis 6:19-20).

teens.question: How is Noah described in Genesis 6:9?
teens.option_b: A just man and perfect in his generations who walked with God
teens.explanation: Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God (Genesis 6:9).

adults.question: What does Genesis 6:2 describe about the "sons of God"?
adults.option_b: They took wives of the daughters of men
adults.explanation: The sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair; and they took them wives (Genesis 6:2).

adults.question: What limit did God set on man's days in Genesis 6:3?
adults.option_b: One hundred and twenty years
adults.explanation: The LORD said, "My spirit shall not always strive with man...his days shall be an hundred and twenty years" (Genesis 6:3).

adults.question: Who are the "giants" (Nephilim) mentioned in Genesis 6:4?
adults.option_a: Fallen angels who lived on earth
adults.option_b: Mighty men of old, men of renown
adults.explanation: There were giants in the earth in those days...mighty men which were of old, men of renown (Genesis 6:4).

adults.question: What does Genesis 6:6 say about God's emotional response to man's wickedness?
adults.option_b: It repented the LORD that he had made man, and it grieved him at his heart
adults.option_c: God was indifferent to their sin
adults.explanation: It repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart (Genesis 6:6).

adults.question: What were the dimensions of the ark in cubits?
adults.option_b: 300 long, 50 wide, 30 high
adults.explanation: The length 300 cubits, the breadth 50 cubits, and the height 30 cubits (Genesis 6:15).

adults.question: How many stories (levels) did the ark have?
adults.option_b: Two
adults.explanation: With lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it (Genesis 6:16).

adults.question: What covenant did God establish with Noah before the flood?
adults.option_b: A covenant to bring Noah and his family safely into the ark
adults.explanation: God said, "With thee will I establish my covenant; and thou shalt come into the ark" (Genesis 6:18).

adults.question: What was Noah instructed to take on the ark besides animals?
adults.option_b: Seeds for planting
adults.option_c: Food for his family and the animals
adults.explanation: God said, "Take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten...for thee and for them" (Genesis 6:21).

kids.question: How many days and nights did it rain during the flood?
kids.explanation: The rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights (Genesis 7:12).

kids.question: How many people were saved on the ark?
kids.explanation: Noah, his wife, his three sons, and their three wives — eight people (Genesis 7:13).

kids.question: Who shut the door of the ark?
kids.explanation: The LORD shut him in (Genesis 7:16).

teens.question: How many of the clean animals did Noah take on the ark?
teens.explanation: Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens, the male and his female (Genesis 7:2).

teens.question: How old was Noah when the flood began?
teens.explanation: Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth (Genesis 7:6).

teens.question: What happened after Noah entered the ark and before the rain started?
teens.explanation: After seven days the waters of the flood were upon the earth (Genesis 7:10).

teens.question: Besides rain, where else did the flood waters come from?
teens.option_b: The fountains of the great deep were broken up
teens.explanation: The fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened (Genesis 7:11).

teens.question: How high did the waters rise above the mountains?
teens.explanation: Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered (Genesis 7:20).

adults.question: On what specific date did the flood begin according to Genesis 7:11?
adults.option_a: The first day of the first month
adults.option_b: The seventeenth day of the second month
adults.option_c: The tenth day of the seventh month
adults.option_d: The first day of the tenth month
adults.explanation: In the second month, the seventeenth day of the month...were all the fountains of the great deep broken up (Genesis 7:11).

adults.question: What is the significance of God shutting the door of the ark in Genesis 7:16?
adults.option_a: The door was too heavy for Noah
adults.option_b: It symbolizes God's sovereign protection and the closing of the opportunity for salvation
adults.option_d: It was a test of faith
adults.explanation: The LORD shut him in (Genesis 7:16) — God himself sealed the ark, indicating divine protection and judgment.

adults.question: Why were clean beasts taken by sevens and unclean by twos?
adults.option_b: Extra clean animals were needed for post-flood sacrifice
adults.explanation: The extra clean animals provided for sacrifice after the flood (Genesis 7:2; cf. 8:20).

adults.question: What phrase in Genesis 7:11 refers to subterranean water sources?
adults.option_b: The fountains of the great deep
adults.explanation: All the fountains of the great deep broken up (Genesis 7:11).

adults.question: How many total days did the waters prevail upon the earth?
adults.option_b: One hundred days
adults.explanation: The waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days (Genesis 7:24).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of only eight people surviving the flood?
adults.option_b: It demonstrates that salvation is for a remnant while judgment falls on the unrepentant
adults.option_d: It fulfilled a prophecy by Enoch
adults.explanation: Only eight souls were saved, illustrating the principle of a faithful remnant (Genesis 7:13; cf. 1 Peter 3:20).

adults.question: What does Genesis 7:16 say about the animals entering the ark?
adults.option_a: Noah herded them in with great difficulty
adults.option_b: They went in male and female as God had commanded Noah
adults.option_d: They ran in from fear of the rain
adults.explanation: They went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him (Genesis 7:16).

adults.question: What specific phrase describes the totality of the destruction outside the ark?
adults.option_b: All flesh died that moved upon the earth
adults.explanation: All flesh died that moved upon the earth, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast (Genesis 7:21).

kids.question: What bird did Noah send out first to check for dry land?
kids.explanation: Noah sent forth a raven, which went to and fro (Genesis 8:7).

kids.question: What did the dove bring back to the ark?
kids.explanation: The dove came back with an olive leaf pluckt off in her mouth (Genesis 8:11).

kids.question: What did Noah build after leaving the ark?
kids.explanation: Noah builded an altar unto the LORD (Genesis 8:20).

teens.question: What mountains did the ark rest upon?
teens.explanation: The ark rested upon the mountains of Ararat (Genesis 8:4).

teens.question: How many times did Noah send out the dove?
teens.explanation: Noah sent the dove three times: first it returned empty, then with an olive leaf, then it did not return (Genesis 8:8-12).

teens.question: What did Noah offer on the altar he built?
teens.option_b: Burnt offerings of every clean beast and fowl
teens.explanation: Noah offered burnt offerings of every clean beast and every clean fowl on the altar (Genesis 8:20).

teens.question: What did God promise in His heart after smelling Noah's offering?
teens.option_a: He would destroy wickedness with fire next time
teens.option_b: He would not again curse the ground for man's sake or destroy every living thing
teens.option_c: He would never allow sin again
teens.explanation: The LORD said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake (Genesis 8:21).

teens.question: What cycle did God promise would never cease?
teens.option_a: The cycle of life and death
teens.option_b: Seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night
teens.option_d: The cycle of floods and droughts
teens.explanation: While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease (Genesis 8:22).

adults.question: On what date did the ark come to rest on the mountains of Ararat?
adults.option_a: The first day of the tenth month
adults.option_b: The seventeenth day of the seventh month
adults.option_c: The first day of the first month
adults.option_d: The twenty-seventh day of the second month
adults.explanation: In the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, the ark rested upon the mountains of Ararat (Genesis 8:4).

adults.question: What did God cause to pass over the earth to help dry the waters?
adults.option_b: A wind
adults.explanation: God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters assuaged (Genesis 8:1).

adults.question: How does the raven's behavior differ from the dove's on the first sending?
adults.option_a: The raven came back immediately; the dove did not return
adults.option_b: The raven went to and fro until waters dried; the dove returned finding no rest
adults.option_d: The raven drowned; the dove flew far away
adults.explanation: The raven went forth to and fro; the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot and returned (Genesis 8:7-9).

adults.question: What does God acknowledge about man's heart in Genesis 8:21 even after the flood?
adults.option_b: The imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth
adults.explanation: The imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth (Genesis 8:21).

adults.question: How long was Noah in the ark from the time the flood began to the time he exited?
adults.option_b: About 265 days
adults.explanation: Comparing Genesis 7:11 and 8:14, Noah was in the ark roughly one year and ten days.

adults.question: What did God "remember" in Genesis 8:1 that initiated the receding of waters?
adults.option_b: Noah, and every living thing in the ark
adults.explanation: God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the cattle that was with him in the ark (Genesis 8:1).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Noah's altar and sacrifice after the flood?
adults.option_a: It was merely a cultural tradition
adults.option_b: It represents worship and thanksgiving, and prefigures the principle of substitutionary sacrifice
adults.option_c: It was required to complete the flood narrative
adults.explanation: Noah's sacrifice of clean animals on an altar foreshadows the Levitical sacrificial system (Genesis 8:20).

adults.question: What command did God give Noah upon leaving the ark?
adults.option_b: Go forth and multiply upon the earth
adults.option_d: Divide the land among his sons
adults.explanation: God said, "Go forth of the ark...bring forth every living thing...that they may breed abundantly" (Genesis 8:16-17).

kids.question: What sign did God put in the sky as a promise?
kids.explanation: God set a rainbow in the cloud as a sign of His covenant (Genesis 9:13).

kids.question: What did God promise with the rainbow?
kids.option_b: He would never flood the whole earth again
kids.explanation: God promised He would never again destroy all flesh with a flood (Genesis 9:15).

kids.question: What did Noah plant after the flood?
kids.explanation: Noah began to be an husbandman, and he planted a vineyard (Genesis 9:20).

teens.question: What new permission did God give mankind regarding food after the flood?
teens.option_b: They could now eat meat as well as plants
teens.option_d: They could eat fruit from any tree
teens.explanation: Every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things (Genesis 9:3).

teens.question: What restriction did God place on eating meat?
teens.option_b: They must not eat the blood
teens.option_c: It must be from clean animals only
teens.option_d: It must be offered to God first
teens.explanation: Flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat (Genesis 9:4).

teens.question: What did God say about anyone who sheds man's blood?
teens.option_b: By man shall his blood be shed, for in the image of God made he man
teens.option_c: They must flee to another land
teens.explanation: Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man (Genesis 9:6).

teens.question: Which of Noah's sons saw his father's nakedness?
teens.explanation: Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father and told his two brethren (Genesis 9:22).

teens.question: Who was cursed because of Ham's disrespect?
teens.explanation: Noah said, "Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be" (Genesis 9:25).

adults.question: With whom did God establish the Noahic covenant?
adults.option_b: Noah, his descendants, and every living creature
adults.explanation: God said, "I establish my covenant with you, and with your seed after you, and with every living creature" (Genesis 9:9-10).

adults.question: What makes the Noahic covenant unique among biblical covenants?
adults.option_b: It is unconditional and universal, applying to all living creatures
adults.explanation: The Noahic covenant is unconditional — God promises never to destroy the earth by flood regardless of human behavior (Genesis 9:11).

adults.question: What does Genesis 9:6 establish as a foundational principle of justice?
adults.option_b: Capital punishment for murder because man is made in God's image
adults.explanation: Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man (Genesis 9:6).

adults.question: How did Shem and Japheth cover their father's nakedness?
adults.option_a: They looked away and called for help
adults.option_b: They walked backward with a garment so they would not see him
adults.option_c: They sent Ham to cover him
adults.explanation: Shem and Japheth took a garment, laid it upon both their shoulders, and went backward, and covered their father (Genesis 9:23).

adults.question: What blessing did Noah pronounce on Shem?
adults.option_a: Shem would be the greatest warrior
adults.option_b: Blessed be the LORD God of Shem
adults.option_c: Shem would inherit all the earth
adults.explanation: Noah said, "Blessed be the LORD God of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant" (Genesis 9:26).

adults.question: What did Noah say about Japheth in his blessing?
adults.option_b: God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem
adults.explanation: God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem (Genesis 9:27).

adults.question: How old was Noah when he died?
adults.option_b: Nine hundred and fifty years
adults.explanation: All the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years: and he died (Genesis 9:29).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the prohibition against eating blood in Genesis 9:4?
adults.option_a: It was merely a dietary health rule
adults.option_b: Blood represents life, which belongs to God, foreshadowing the sacredness of blood atonement
adults.option_c: It was a temporary cultural practice
adults.option_d: God disliked the taste of blood
adults.explanation: The prohibition against eating blood (Genesis 9:4) establishes the principle that the life is in the blood, foundational to later atonement theology (cf. Leviticus 17:11).

kids.question: How many sons did Noah have?
kids.explanation: Noah had three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth (Genesis 10:1).

kids.question: Who was called a mighty hunter before the LORD?
kids.explanation: Nimrod was a mighty hunter before the LORD (Genesis 10:9).

kids.question: What does Genesis 10 tell us about Noah's grandsons?
kids.option_b: They became the nations of the earth
kids.explanation: By these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood (Genesis 10:32).

teens.question: Genesis 10 is commonly known as what?
teens.explanation: Genesis 10 is known as the Table of Nations — it lists the descendants of Noah's sons and the nations they founded.

teens.question: Whose descendants include Nimrod?
teens.explanation: Cush begat Nimrod — Cush was a son of Ham (Genesis 10:6, 8).

teens.question: What land did Nimrod begin his kingdom in?
teens.explanation: The beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar (Genesis 10:10).

teens.question: Which of Noah's sons is the ancestor of the Semitic peoples?
teens.explanation: Shem is the ancestor of the Semitic peoples, including Abraham and the Israelites (Genesis 10:21-31).

teens.question: What great cities did Nimrod build or establish?
teens.explanation: Nimrod built Babel, Erech, Accad, Calneh in Shinar, and went to Assyria and built Nineveh (Genesis 10:10-11).

adults.question: From which son of Noah are the Japhethite peoples descended, and what regions did they spread to?
adults.option_b: Japheth — they spread to the coastlands and isles of the Gentiles
adults.option_d: Canaan — they spread to the Levant
adults.explanation: By these were the isles of the Gentiles divided in their lands (Genesis 10:5) — Japheth's descendants.

adults.question: Who was Eber, and why is he significant?
adults.option_a: He was a son of Ham who settled Egypt
adults.option_b: He was a descendant of Shem from whom the name "Hebrew" likely derives
adults.option_c: He was a builder of Babel
adults.option_d: He was a priest of the Most High God
adults.explanation: Eber was a descendant of Shem (Genesis 10:21, 24). The name "Hebrew" is thought to derive from Eber.

adults.question: What does the phrase "a mighty hunter before the LORD" likely imply about Nimrod?
adults.option_a: He was a godly man who hunted for food
adults.option_b: He was a powerful ruler and conqueror in defiance of God
adults.option_c: He hunted only with God's permission
adults.option_d: He was a priest who offered animal sacrifices
adults.explanation: Nimrod was a mighty hunter before the LORD (Genesis 10:9) — traditionally understood as a powerful, possibly defiant ruler.

adults.question: Which peoples descended from Canaan according to Genesis 10?
adults.option_b: The Jebusites, Amorites, Hivites, and other Canaanite nations
adults.explanation: Canaan begat Sidon, Heth, the Jebusite, the Amorite, the Girgasite, the Hivite, etc. (Genesis 10:15-18).

adults.question: What does Genesis 10:25 say happened in the days of Peleg?
adults.option_b: The earth was divided
adults.option_d: The tower of Babel was completed
adults.explanation: Unto Eber were born two sons: the name of one was Peleg; for in his days was the earth divided (Genesis 10:25).

adults.question: From Ham's son Mizraim, which nation descended?
adults.option_b: Assyria
adults.explanation: Mizraim is the Hebrew name for Egypt. Ham begat Mizraim (Genesis 10:6).

adults.question: How does Genesis 10 organize the nations?
adults.option_b: By their families, tongues, lands, and nations
adults.explanation: These are the families of the sons of Noah, after their generations, in their nations (Genesis 10:32).

adults.question: What is the theological purpose of the Table of Nations in Genesis 10?
adults.option_a: It is merely a historical record
adults.option_b: It shows God's sovereignty over all nations and sets the stage for the Babel narrative and Abrahamic call
adults.option_c: It proves the flood was local
adults.option_d: It lists the enemies of Israel
adults.explanation: The Table of Nations shows how all humanity descended from one family, setting the stage for Babel (ch. 11) and God's call of Abraham (ch. 12).

kids.question: What did the people try to build that reached up to heaven?
kids.explanation: They said, "Let us build us a city and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven" (Genesis 11:4).

kids.question: What did God do to stop the people from building the tower?
kids.option_b: He confused their languages so they couldn't understand each other
kids.explanation: The LORD did confound the language of all the earth (Genesis 11:9).

kids.question: What was the name of the place where the tower was built?
kids.explanation: Therefore is the name of it called Babel (Genesis 11:9).

teens.question: What was the whole earth like before the Tower of Babel?
teens.option_b: Of one language and one speech
teens.explanation: The whole earth was of one language, and of one speech (Genesis 11:1).

teens.question: Why did the people want to build the tower?
teens.option_a: To worship God at a high place
teens.option_b: To make a name for themselves and avoid being scattered
teens.option_c: To protect themselves from another flood
teens.option_d: To see the stars more closely
teens.explanation: They said, "Let us make us a name, lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth" (Genesis 11:4).

teens.question: What material did they use to build the tower instead of stone?
teens.explanation: They had brick for stone, and slime had they for morter (Genesis 11:3).

teens.question: Who was Abram's father?
teens.explanation: Terah begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran (Genesis 11:26).

teens.question: Where did Terah originally take his family before settling in Haran?
teens.option_a: He set out for the land of Canaan
teens.explanation: They went forth to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there (Genesis 11:31).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of God saying "let us go down" in Genesis 11:7?
adults.option_a: God needed to travel to the tower
adults.option_b: The plural "us" echoes Genesis 1:26 and suggests the divine plurality
adults.option_c: God was speaking to angels only
adults.option_d: It was a figure of speech
adults.explanation: The LORD said, "Go to, let us go down" — the plural mirrors the creation account's "let us" (Genesis 11:7; cf. 1:26).

adults.question: What does the name "Babel" mean?
adults.option_b: Confusion, because the LORD did there confound the language
adults.explanation: The name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth (Genesis 11:9).

adults.question: What was God's concern about humanity in Genesis 11:6?
adults.option_b: Nothing would be restrained from them if they remained unified in rebellion
adults.option_d: They were too sinful to continue
adults.explanation: The LORD said, "Now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do" (Genesis 11:6).

adults.question: The genealogy in Genesis 11:10-26 traces which lineage?
adults.option_b: Japheth to the Greeks
adults.explanation: The genealogy traces the line from Shem through Eber, Peleg, and others down to Terah and Abram (Genesis 11:10-26).

adults.question: What notable decline is observed in the lifespans recorded in Genesis 11?
adults.option_b: They decrease significantly from Shem (600) to Terah (205)
adults.option_d: They alternate between long and short
adults.explanation: Lifespans decline from Shem (600 years) progressively to Terah (205 years), showing the post-flood decrease.

adults.question: Who was Abram's wife, and what problem is mentioned about her?
adults.option_b: Sarai; she was barren
adults.explanation: Abram's wife was Sarai, and she was barren; she had no child (Genesis 11:29-30).

adults.question: What happened to Abram's brother Haran?
adults.option_b: He died in Ur of the Chaldees before his father Terah
adults.explanation: Haran died before his father Terah in the land of his nativity, in Ur of the Chaldees (Genesis 11:28).

adults.question: How does the Tower of Babel narrative contrast with God's mandate in Genesis 9:1?
adults.option_b: God commanded mankind to fill the earth, but at Babel they refused to scatter
adults.option_c: God commanded them to build cities
adults.option_d: The tower was a form of worship
adults.explanation: God said "be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth" (9:1), but at Babel they said "lest we be scattered" (11:4) — direct defiance.

kids.question: What did God tell Abram to do?
kids.option_b: Leave his country and go to a new land
kids.option_c: Stay home and be a farmer
kids.option_d: Go back to the garden of Eden
kids.explanation: God said, "Get thee out of thy country...unto a land that I will shew thee" (Genesis 12:1).

kids.question: What did God promise to make Abram into?
kids.explanation: God said, "I will make of thee a great nation" (Genesis 12:2).

kids.question: Who went with Abram on his journey?
kids.option_b: His wife Sarai and nephew Lot
kids.explanation: Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son (Genesis 12:5).

teens.question: What three promises did God make to Abram in Genesis 12:2-3?
teens.option_a: A big house, many servants, and long life
teens.option_b: A great nation, a great name, and a blessing to all families of the earth
teens.option_c: A rich kingdom, a palace, and many sons
teens.option_d: Victory in battle, wisdom, and eternal life
teens.explanation: God promised to make Abram a great nation, bless him and make his name great, and bless all families of the earth through him (Genesis 12:2-3).

teens.question: How old was Abram when he left Haran?
teens.explanation: Abram was seventy and five years old when he departed out of Haran (Genesis 12:4).

teens.question: Where did Abram build his first altar in Canaan?
teens.option_b: At the plain of Moreh in Shechem
teens.explanation: Abram passed through the land unto the place of Sichem, unto the plain of Moreh, and there he builded an altar (Genesis 12:6-7).

teens.question: Why did Abram go to Egypt?
teens.option_b: There was a famine in the land
teens.explanation: There was a famine in the land: and Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there (Genesis 12:10).

teens.question: What did Abram ask Sarai to say in Egypt?
teens.explanation: Abram said, "Say, I pray thee, thou art my sister" (Genesis 12:13).

adults.question: What is the theological term for God's promise to Abram in Genesis 12:1-3?
adults.option_b: The Abrahamic Covenant
adults.explanation: God's promises to Abram in Genesis 12:1-3 are the foundation of the Abrahamic Covenant.

adults.question: What does the phrase "I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee" establish?
adults.option_a: A personal protection plan for Abram
adults.option_b: God's protective relationship with Abram and his descendants
adults.explanation: God established a principle of blessing or cursing nations based on their treatment of Abram's line (Genesis 12:3).

adults.question: What phrase in Genesis 12:3 has universal Messianic implications?
adults.option_b: "In thee shall all families of the earth be blessed"
adults.option_c: "I will make of thee a great nation"
adults.option_d: "I will bless them that bless thee"
adults.explanation: "In thee shall all families of the earth be blessed" points to the Messiah coming through Abram's line (Genesis 12:3; cf. Galatians 3:8).

adults.question: What moral problem did Abram's deception about Sarai in Egypt create?
adults.option_b: He endangered Sarai's virtue and showed lack of faith in God's promise to protect him
adults.option_c: He was punished by God immediately
adults.explanation: Abram's half-truth endangered Sarai and showed a failure of faith in God's protection (Genesis 12:11-13).

adults.question: What did Pharaoh do when he discovered the truth about Sarai?
adults.option_b: He rebuked Abram and sent him away with his wife and possessions
adults.explanation: Pharaoh called Abram and said, "What is this thou hast done unto me?" and sent him away (Genesis 12:18-20).

adults.question: What plagues did God send upon Pharaoh's house?
adults.option_b: Great plagues because of Sarai
adults.explanation: The LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai (Genesis 12:17).

adults.question: Who were the Canaanites mentioned as being in the land when Abram arrived?
adults.option_b: Descendants of Canaan, son of Ham
adults.explanation: The Canaanite was then in the land (Genesis 12:6) — descendants of Canaan, son of Ham.

adults.question: What did God specifically promise Abram at Shechem?
adults.option_a: I will make you a king
adults.option_b: Unto thy seed will I give this land
adults.option_d: Your wife will bear a son this year
adults.explanation: The LORD appeared unto Abram, and said, Unto thy seed will I give this land (Genesis 12:7).

kids.question: Why did Abram and Lot need to separate?
kids.option_b: Their herds were too large to share the same land
kids.explanation: The land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together, for their substance was great (Genesis 13:6).

kids.question: Which direction did Lot choose to go?
kids.explanation: Lot chose him all the plain of Jordan (Genesis 13:11).

kids.question: What did God promise to give Abram?
kids.option_b: All the land he could see
kids.explanation: God said, "All the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it" (Genesis 13:15).

teens.question: What was the conflict between Abram's and Lot's herdsmen about?
teens.option_b: The land could not support both of their large flocks
teens.explanation: There was strife between the herdmen because the land was not able to bear them both (Genesis 13:6-7).

teens.question: What generous offer did Abram make to Lot?
teens.option_a: He offered Lot money to leave
teens.option_b: He let Lot choose which land he wanted first
teens.option_c: He gave Lot half his flock
teens.option_d: He built Lot a new home
teens.explanation: Abram said, "If thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right" — he gave Lot first choice (Genesis 13:9).

teens.question: What was the plain of Jordan compared to in Genesis 13:10?
teens.option_b: The garden of the LORD and the land of Egypt
teens.explanation: The plain of Jordan was well watered every where...as the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt (Genesis 13:10).

teens.question: Near what wicked city did Lot choose to live?
teens.explanation: Lot dwelled in the cities of the plain, and pitched his tent toward Sodom (Genesis 13:12).

teens.question: What did God tell Abram to do to claim the land?
teens.option_b: Walk through the length and breadth of it
teens.explanation: God said, "Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it; for I will give it unto thee" (Genesis 13:17).

adults.question: What does Genesis 13:13 reveal about the men of Sodom?
adults.option_b: They were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly
adults.explanation: The men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly (Genesis 13:13).

adults.question: How did God describe the number of Abram's future descendants?
adults.option_a: As many as the fish in the sea
adults.option_b: As the dust of the earth — innumerable
adults.option_c: As many as the trees in the forest
adults.option_d: As the rivers flowing to the sea
adults.explanation: God said, "I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth" (Genesis 13:16).

adults.question: Where did Abram settle after separating from Lot?
adults.option_b: In the plain of Mamre in Hebron
adults.explanation: Abram dwelled in the plain of Mamre, which is in Hebron, and built there an altar unto the LORD (Genesis 13:18).

adults.question: What character flaw does Lot's choice of the well-watered plain reveal?
adults.option_b: He chose based on outward appearance and material gain rather than spiritual wisdom
adults.explanation: Lot chose based on what looked good — the well-watered plain — without considering the wickedness of Sodom (Genesis 13:10-13).

adults.question: In what four directions did God tell Abram to look to see the promised land?
adults.option_b: Northward, southward, eastward, westward
adults.explanation: God said, "Lift up thine eyes, and look from the place where thou art northward, and southward, and eastward, and westward" (Genesis 13:14).

adults.question: What was the time frame of God's land promise to Abram?
adults.option_b: For ever
adults.option_c: Until the coming of the Messiah
adults.explanation: God said, "To thee will I give it, and to thy seed for ever" (Genesis 13:15).

adults.question: What does Abram's willingness to let Lot choose first demonstrate?
adults.option_b: Faith that God would provide regardless of Lot's choice
adults.option_d: Desire to avoid conflict at any cost
adults.explanation: Abram's generosity shows trust in God's promise — he didn't need to grasp for the best portion (Genesis 13:8-9).

adults.question: What act of worship did Abram perform at Hebron?
adults.option_a: He offered a burnt offering of sheep
adults.option_b: He built an altar unto the LORD
adults.explanation: Abram came and dwelt in the plain of Mamre, in Hebron, and built there an altar unto the LORD (Genesis 13:18).

kids.question: What happened to Lot in this chapter?
kids.option_b: He was captured in a war
kids.explanation: They took Lot, Abram's brother's son, who dwelt in Sodom, and his goods (Genesis 14:12).

kids.question: What did Abram do when he heard Lot was captured?
kids.option_b: He gathered his men and rescued Lot
kids.option_d: He waited for God to help
kids.explanation: When Abram heard his brother was taken captive, he armed his trained servants and pursued them (Genesis 14:14).

kids.question: Who blessed Abram after the battle?
kids.explanation: Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine and blessed Abram (Genesis 14:18-19).

teens.question: How many trained servants did Abram arm to rescue Lot?
teens.explanation: Abram armed his trained servants, born in his own house, three hundred and eighteen (Genesis 14:14).

teens.question: Who was Melchizedek?
teens.option_b: King of Salem and priest of the most high God
teens.explanation: Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine: and he was the priest of the most high God (Genesis 14:18).

teens.question: What did Abram give to Melchizedek?
teens.option_b: Tithes of all (a tenth of everything)
teens.explanation: Abram gave him tithes of all (Genesis 14:20).

teens.question: What did the king of Sodom offer Abram after the battle?
teens.option_b: The goods and spoils of war
teens.explanation: The king of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the persons, and take the goods to thyself" (Genesis 14:21).

teens.question: Why did Abram refuse the king of Sodom's offer?
teens.option_b: So the king could not say he had made Abram rich
teens.option_d: God told him not to accept
teens.explanation: Abram said, "Lest thou shouldest say, I have made Abram rich" (Genesis 14:23).

adults.question: Which king led the coalition of four kings against the five kings of the Jordan plain?
adults.option_b: Chedorlaomer king of Elam
adults.explanation: The four kings made war with the five kings, with Chedorlaomer as the dominant king (Genesis 14:1-4).

adults.question: How long had the five kings served Chedorlaomer before rebelling?
adults.option_b: Ten years
adults.option_c: Twelve years, and rebelled in the thirteenth
adults.explanation: Twelve years they served Chedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled (Genesis 14:4).

adults.question: What geographical feature did the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fall into during battle?
adults.option_b: Slimepits (tar pits)
adults.explanation: The vale of Siddim was full of slimepits; and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fell there (Genesis 14:10).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Melchizedek being both king and priest?
adults.option_a: He was simply a local ruler with religious duties
adults.option_b: He is a type of Christ who is both King and Priest, as referenced in Psalm 110 and Hebrews 7
adults.option_d: He was an angel in disguise
adults.explanation: Melchizedek as king-priest prefigures Christ (Genesis 14:18; cf. Psalm 110:4, Hebrews 7:1-3).

adults.question: What does "Salem" mean, and what city is it believed to be?
adults.option_a: Victory — it is believed to be Babylon
adults.option_b: Peace — it is believed to be Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Strength — it is believed to be Samaria
adults.option_d: Holy — it is believed to be Hebron
adults.explanation: Salem means "peace" and is traditionally identified with Jerusalem (Genesis 14:18; cf. Psalm 76:2).

adults.question: What oath did Abram swear to the king of Sodom?
adults.option_a: He swore by his own name
adults.option_b: He lifted his hand unto the LORD, the most high God, that he would take nothing
adults.option_c: He swore by the stars of heaven
adults.explanation: Abram said, "I have lift up mine hand unto the LORD, the most high God...that I will not take any thing" (Genesis 14:22-23).

adults.question: What does Abram's tithing to Melchizedek establish?
adults.option_a: That taxes must be paid to kings
adults.option_b: The principle of tithing predates the Mosaic Law
adults.explanation: Abram's tithe to Melchizedek shows the practice of giving a tenth existed before the Law of Moses (Genesis 14:20).

adults.question: Who did Abram say could take their portion of the spoils?
adults.option_b: The young men and his allies Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre
adults.explanation: Abram said, "Save only that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men which went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre" (Genesis 14:24).

kids.question: What did God tell Abram to count?
kids.explanation: God said, "Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars...so shall thy seed be" (Genesis 15:5).

kids.question: What did God promise Abram he would have?
kids.option_b: More children than he could count
kids.explanation: God promised Abram's descendants would be as numerous as the stars (Genesis 15:5).

kids.question: What did God say He was to Abram?
kids.explanation: God said, "I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward" (Genesis 15:1).

teens.question: What was Abram's concern in this chapter?
teens.option_a: He was afraid of his enemies
teens.option_b: He had no children, and his servant Eliezer would inherit
teens.option_c: He was running out of food
teens.option_d: He wanted to return to Haran
teens.explanation: Abram said, "I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus" (Genesis 15:2).

teens.question: What did Abram do when God promised him descendants as numerous as the stars?
teens.explanation: Abram believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness (Genesis 15:6).

teens.question: How did God count Abram's faith?
teens.explanation: He believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness (Genesis 15:6).

teens.question: What did God tell Abram would happen to his descendants before they inherited the land?
teens.option_a: They would wander the desert for 40 years
teens.option_b: They would be strangers and servants in a land not theirs for 400 years
teens.option_d: They would live in peace for 100 years
teens.explanation: God said, "Thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs, and shall serve them...four hundred years" (Genesis 15:13).

teens.question: What strange vision did Abram see during the covenant ceremony?
teens.option_b: A smoking furnace and a burning lamp passing between the pieces
teens.explanation: A smoking furnace, and a burning lamp passed between those pieces (Genesis 15:17).

adults.question: What key theological doctrine is established by Genesis 15:6?
adults.option_b: Justification by faith — Abram believed God and it was counted as righteousness
adults.explanation: Genesis 15:6 establishes justification by faith, cited by Paul in Romans 4:3 and Galatians 3:6.

adults.question: What animals did God instruct Abram to bring for the covenant ceremony?
adults.option_b: A heifer, a she goat, a ram (each three years old), a turtledove, and a young pigeon
adults.explanation: God said, "Take me an heifer of three years old, and a she goat of three years old, and a ram of three years old, and a turtledove, and a young pigeon" (Genesis 15:9).

adults.question: What did Abram do with the animals?
adults.option_a: He burned them as a sacrifice
adults.option_b: He divided them in the midst and laid each piece opposite the other
adults.option_c: He released them into the field
adults.option_d: He kept them as a flock
adults.explanation: He took all these and divided them in the midst, and laid each piece one against another; but the birds divided he not (Genesis 15:10).

adults.question: What is the significance of God alone passing between the pieces in Genesis 15:17?
adults.option_a: It was a standard ancient practice with no special meaning
adults.option_b: It shows the covenant is unconditional — God alone binds Himself, not requiring Abram's performance
adults.option_c: It means Abram failed the test
adults.option_d: It was a vision with no real meaning
adults.explanation: In ancient covenant ritual, both parties walked between pieces. God alone passing through indicates an unconditional, one-sided covenant (Genesis 15:17).

adults.question: What did God prophesy about the nation that would enslave Abram's descendants?
adults.option_a: They would be destroyed by famine
adults.option_b: God would judge that nation, and Abram's seed would come out with great substance
adults.option_c: They would become allies of Israel
adults.option_d: They would convert to worship God
adults.explanation: That nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge: and afterward shall they come out with great substance (Genesis 15:14).

adults.question: What reason does God give for the delay in giving the land to Abram's seed?
adults.option_a: The land was not yet fertile
adults.option_b: The iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full
adults.option_c: Abram's family was not large enough
adults.explanation: The iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full (Genesis 15:16) — God delays judgment until sin reaches its fullness.

adults.question: What fell upon Abram as the sun was going down?
adults.option_b: A deep sleep and a horror of great darkness
adults.explanation: When the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell upon Abram; and, lo, an horror of great darkness fell upon him (Genesis 15:12).

adults.question: What geographic boundaries did God define for the promised land in Genesis 15:18?
adults.option_b: From the river of Egypt unto the great river Euphrates
adults.option_c: From the Mediterranean to the Jordan
adults.option_d: From Mount Sinai to Mount Hermon
adults.explanation: God said, "Unto thy seed have I given this land, from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates" (Genesis 15:18).

kids.question: Who was Sarai's servant?
kids.explanation: Sarai had an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar (Genesis 16:1).

kids.question: What was the name of Hagar's son?
kids.explanation: The angel of the LORD said, "Thou shalt call his name Ishmael" (Genesis 16:11).

kids.question: Where did the angel find Hagar when she ran away?
kids.option_b: By a fountain of water in the wilderness
kids.explanation: The angel of the LORD found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness (Genesis 16:7).

teens.question: Why did Sarai give Hagar to Abram?
teens.option_b: Sarai was barren and hoped to have children through Hagar
teens.explanation: Sarai said, "The LORD hath restrained me from bearing: I pray thee, go in unto my maid; it may be that I may obtain children by her" (Genesis 16:2).

teens.question: What happened between Sarai and Hagar after Hagar became pregnant?
teens.option_b: Hagar despised Sarai, and Sarai dealt harshly with her
teens.explanation: When Hagar saw she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes, and Sarai dealt hardly with her (Genesis 16:4-6).

teens.question: What does the name Ishmael mean?
teens.explanation: Ishmael means "God shall hear" — because the LORD hath heard thy affliction (Genesis 16:11).

teens.question: What kind of man did the angel say Ishmael would be?
teens.option_b: A wild man, his hand against every man
teens.explanation: He will be a wild man; his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him (Genesis 16:12).

teens.question: What did Hagar call the LORD who spoke to her?
teens.explanation: She called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me (Genesis 16:13).

adults.question: What does Sarai's plan to use Hagar as a surrogate reflect about her faith?
adults.option_b: An attempt to fulfill God's promise through human means rather than waiting on God
adults.explanation: Sarai's scheme reflects impatience with God's timing and an attempt to fulfill the promise through human effort (Genesis 16:1-2).

adults.question: Who is the "angel of the LORD" that appears to Hagar, and why is this significant?
adults.option_b: This is the first appearance of the angel of the LORD in Scripture, often understood as a theophany
adults.explanation: Genesis 16:7 is the first occurrence of "the angel of the LORD" in the Bible, a figure many scholars identify as a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ.

adults.question: What did the angel tell Hagar to do?
adults.option_b: Return to her mistress and submit herself
adults.option_d: Wait by the well until Abram came
adults.explanation: The angel said, "Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands" (Genesis 16:9).

adults.question: What promise did the angel make about Hagar's descendants?
adults.option_a: They would be few but powerful
adults.option_b: They would be multiplied exceedingly so they cannot be numbered
adults.explanation: The angel said, "I will multiply thy seed exceedingly, that it shall not be numbered for multitude" (Genesis 16:10).

adults.question: What does the name Beer-lahai-roi mean?
adults.option_a: The well of the living God
adults.option_b: The well of him that liveth and seeth me
adults.explanation: The well was called Beer-lahai-roi — "the well of him that liveth and seeth me" (Genesis 16:14).

adults.question: How old was Abram when Ishmael was born?
adults.option_b: Eighty years old
adults.explanation: Abram was fourscore and six years old, when Hagar bare Ishmael (Genesis 16:16).

adults.question: What does Genesis 16:12 prophesy about Ishmael's dwelling?
adults.option_b: He shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren
adults.option_d: He would live alone in the desert
adults.explanation: He shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren (Genesis 16:12).

adults.question: How does Hagar's experience at the well foreshadow themes in Scripture?
adults.option_b: God sees and hears the afflicted, a theme echoed in the Exodus and Psalms
adults.option_d: It only applies to Hagar's situation
adults.explanation: Hagar's naming of God as "the God who sees me" establishes the theme that God sees and hears the oppressed (Genesis 16:13).

kids.question: What new name did God give Abram?
kids.explanation: God said, "Thy name shall be Abraham; for a father of many nations have I made thee" (Genesis 17:5).

kids.question: What new name did God give Sarai?
kids.explanation: God said, "Thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be" (Genesis 17:15).

kids.question: What son did God promise Abraham and Sarah would have?
kids.explanation: God said, "Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son indeed; and thou shalt call his name Isaac" (Genesis 17:19).

teens.question: What does the name Abraham mean?
teens.explanation: God changed his name to Abraham, meaning "father of many nations" (Genesis 17:5).

teens.question: What sign of the covenant did God institute in this chapter?
teens.explanation: God said, "Every man child among you shall be circumcised...it shall be a token of the covenant" (Genesis 17:10-11).

teens.question: At what age were males to be circumcised?
teens.explanation: He that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you (Genesis 17:12).

teens.question: How did Abraham react when God said Sarah would have a son?
teens.option_b: He fell on his face and laughed
teens.explanation: Abraham fell upon his face, and laughed, and said, "Shall a child be born unto him that is an hundred years old?" (Genesis 17:17).

teens.question: How old was Abraham when he was circumcised?
teens.explanation: Abraham was ninety years old and nine, when he was circumcised (Genesis 17:24).

adults.question: By what name does God reveal Himself to Abraham in Genesis 17:1?
adults.option_b: El Elyon
adults.explanation: The LORD appeared to Abram, and said, "I am the Almighty God (El Shaddai)" (Genesis 17:1).

adults.question: What command did God give Abraham along with the name El Shaddai?
adults.option_b: Walk before me, and be thou perfect
adults.explanation: God said, "Walk before me, and be thou perfect" (Genesis 17:1).

adults.question: What would be the penalty for an uncircumcised male in Israel?
adults.option_b: That soul shall be cut off from his people for breaking the covenant
adults.option_c: He would be exiled for a year
adults.explanation: The uncircumcised man child whose flesh is not circumcised, that soul shall be cut off from his people; he hath broken my covenant (Genesis 17:14).

adults.question: What did God promise specifically regarding Ishmael in response to Abraham's plea?
adults.option_b: God would bless Ishmael, make him fruitful, and make him the father of twelve princes
adults.option_c: Ishmael would be sent away immediately
adults.explanation: God said, "I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful...twelve princes shall he beget" (Genesis 17:20).

adults.question: With whom did God say He would establish His covenant — Isaac or Ishmael?
adults.option_b: Isaac, whom Sarah would bear
adults.explanation: God said, "My covenant will I establish with Isaac, which Sarah shall bear unto thee" (Genesis 17:21).

adults.question: What does the name Isaac mean, and why?
adults.option_b: Laughter — because both Abraham and Sarah laughed
adults.option_c: Blessing — because he was a blessed child
adults.option_d: Miracle — because his birth was miraculous
adults.explanation: Isaac means "he laughs" — Abraham laughed (17:17) and Sarah later laughed (18:12) at the promise.

adults.question: How old was Ishmael when he was circumcised?
adults.option_b: Ten years old
adults.explanation: Ishmael his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised (Genesis 17:25).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of circumcision as a covenant sign?
adults.option_a: It was merely a health practice
adults.option_b: It was an outward sign of an inward covenant relationship, applied to the organ of reproduction to symbolize the covenant passing through generations
adults.option_c: It was a punishment for sin
adults.explanation: Circumcision as a sign in the flesh of the reproductive organ symbolizes the covenant being passed through Abraham's seed (Genesis 17:11).

kids.question: How many visitors came to see Abraham at his tent?
kids.explanation: The LORD appeared unto him...and, lo, three men stood by him (Genesis 18:1-2).

kids.question: What did Sarah do when she heard she would have a baby?
kids.explanation: Sarah laughed within herself (Genesis 18:12).

kids.question: What famous question did God ask about Sarah's laughter?
kids.option_b: Is any thing too hard for the LORD?
kids.explanation: The LORD said, "Is any thing too hard for the LORD?" (Genesis 18:14).

teens.question: Where did the three visitors appear to Abraham?
teens.option_c: At the gate of a city
teens.explanation: The LORD appeared unto him in the plains of Mamre; and he sat in the tent door (Genesis 18:1).

teens.question: How did Abraham treat the three visitors?
teens.option_b: He ran to meet them, bowed, washed their feet, and prepared a meal
teens.explanation: Abraham ran to meet them, bowed, offered water, and prepared a feast (Genesis 18:2-8).

teens.question: Why did Sarah laugh?
teens.option_a: She was happy about a joke
teens.option_b: She thought she was too old to have a child
teens.explanation: Sarah laughed, saying, "After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?" (Genesis 18:12).

teens.question: What did Abraham plead with God about regarding Sodom?
teens.option_a: To let him rescue Lot first
teens.option_b: To spare the city if righteous people were found there
teens.explanation: Abraham interceded, asking God to spare Sodom for the sake of the righteous (Genesis 18:23-32).

teens.question: What was the lowest number of righteous people Abraham bargained down to?
teens.explanation: Abraham bargained from fifty down to ten righteous people (Genesis 18:32).

adults.question: What is the significance of Abraham offering a calf, butter, milk, and cakes to his visitors?
adults.option_b: It demonstrates lavish Near Eastern hospitality and honor given to the divine visitors
adults.option_c: He was showing off his wealth
adults.option_d: It was a required religious offering
adults.explanation: Abraham prepared a generous feast, reflecting the highest standard of ancient hospitality (Genesis 18:6-8).

adults.question: What did the LORD say about why He would not hide His plans from Abraham?
adults.option_b: Abraham would become a great nation and all nations would be blessed through him
adults.option_d: It was common for God to share His plans
adults.explanation: The LORD said, "Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do, seeing that Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation?" (Genesis 18:17-18).

adults.question: What does God say is the reason He chose Abraham in Genesis 18:19?
adults.option_b: That he would command his children to keep the way of the LORD, to do justice and judgment
adults.option_c: Because Abraham was the oldest patriarch
adults.option_d: Because Abraham offered the best sacrifices
adults.explanation: God said, "I know him, that he will command his children...to keep the way of the LORD, to do justice and judgment" (Genesis 18:19).

adults.question: What key attribute of God does Abraham appeal to in his intercession for Sodom?
adults.option_b: God's justice — "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?"
adults.explanation: Abraham said, "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?" (Genesis 18:25).

adults.question: What does Sarah's denial of laughing and God's response reveal?
adults.option_b: Even after divine revelation, human weakness (fear and denial) persists, and God sees through it
adults.explanation: Sarah denied, saying, "I laughed not." And he said, "Nay; but thou didst laugh" (Genesis 18:15).

adults.question: How does Abraham's intercession for Sodom reflect the biblical role of intercessory prayer?
adults.option_b: It establishes the pattern of a righteous person interceding for others, a theme that runs through Moses, Samuel, and Christ
adults.explanation: Abraham's bold intercession foreshadows the intercessory role of Moses (Exodus 32) and ultimately Christ (Genesis 18:23-32).

adults.question: What had the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah become according to God?
adults.option_b: Great, and their sin very grievous
adults.explanation: The LORD said, "The cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and their sin is very grievous" (Genesis 18:20).

adults.question: What does the phrase "Is any thing too hard for the LORD?" in Genesis 18:14 establish theologically?
adults.option_b: The absolute omnipotence of God — nothing is beyond His power
adults.option_d: That God works only through natural means
adults.explanation: "Is any thing too hard for the LORD?" declares God's omnipotence and is echoed in Jeremiah 32:17, 27 (Genesis 18:14).

kids.question: What two cities did God destroy because of their wickedness?
kids.explanation: The LORD rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire (Genesis 19:24).

kids.question: Who did the angels help escape from Sodom?
kids.explanation: The angels brought Lot, his wife, and two daughters out of the city (Genesis 19:16).

kids.question: What happened to Lot's wife when she looked back?
kids.option_b: She became a pillar of salt
kids.explanation: His wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt (Genesis 19:26).

teens.question: Who visited Lot in Sodom?
teens.explanation: There came two angels to Sodom at even; and Lot sat in the gate of Sodom (Genesis 19:1).

teens.question: What did the men of Sodom want to do to Lot's visitors?
teens.option_b: Harm them and commit wicked acts against them
teens.explanation: The men of Sodom demanded Lot bring them out "that we may know them" — a demand for violent wickedness (Genesis 19:5).

teens.question: How did the angels protect Lot from the mob?
teens.option_b: They struck the men with blindness
teens.explanation: The angels smote the men that were at the door with blindness (Genesis 19:11).

teens.question: What warning did the angels give Lot about the city?
teens.option_b: The LORD will destroy this place
teens.explanation: The angels said, "We will destroy this place, because the cry of them is waxen great before the face of the LORD" (Genesis 19:13).

teens.question: Where did the angels tell Lot to flee to?
teens.explanation: The angels said, "Escape to the mountain, lest thou be consumed" (Genesis 19:17).

adults.question: What does Lot's position "sitting in the gate of Sodom" suggest about his status?
adults.option_b: He held a position of civic authority or prominence
adults.explanation: Sitting in the gate was a sign of civic leadership in ancient cities (Genesis 19:1).

adults.question: What small city did Lot plead to flee to instead of the mountain?
adults.option_b: Zoar
adults.explanation: Lot said, "This city is near to flee unto, and it is a little one." The name of it was Zoar (Genesis 19:20-22).

adults.question: What fell upon Sodom and Gomorrah from heaven?
adults.option_b: Brimstone and fire from the LORD out of heaven
adults.explanation: The LORD rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire from the LORD out of heaven (Genesis 19:24).

adults.question: What did Abraham see when he looked toward Sodom the next morning?
adults.option_b: The smoke of the country went up as the smoke of a furnace
adults.explanation: Abraham looked toward Sodom...and, lo, the smoke of the country went up as the smoke of a furnace (Genesis 19:28).

adults.question: Why is Lot's wife becoming a pillar of salt theologically significant?
adults.option_a: Salt was valuable in that era
adults.option_b: It illustrates the consequence of disobedience and longing for the sinful world one is told to leave behind
adults.option_c: It was a natural chemical reaction
adults.explanation: Lot's wife looking back symbolizes attachment to sin and disobedience to God's command; Jesus references it in Luke 17:32.

adults.question: What does Genesis 19:29 say was the reason Lot was saved?
adults.option_b: God remembered Abraham and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow
adults.option_d: The angels chose to save him
adults.explanation: God remembered Abraham, and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow (Genesis 19:29).

adults.question: What troubling events occurred after Lot fled to the cave in the mountain?
adults.option_b: His daughters got him drunk and bore children by him, giving rise to Moab and Ammon
adults.option_c: He offered sacrifice and worshipped God
adults.explanation: Lot's daughters bore sons: Moab (father of the Moabites) and Ben-ammi (father of the Ammonites) (Genesis 19:36-38).

adults.question: How does the destruction of Sodom serve as a theological archetype in Scripture?
adults.option_b: It becomes the standard example of divine judgment on extreme wickedness, referenced by prophets and by Jesus
adults.option_c: It only applies to that specific situation
adults.option_d: It was exaggerated in later traditions
