adults.explanation: Sodom's destruction is referenced as a paradigm of judgment in Isaiah 1:9, Ezekiel 16:49, Jude 1:7, and by Jesus in Matthew 10:15.

kids.question: What king took Sarah because Abraham said she was his sister?
kids.explanation: Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah (Genesis 20:2).

kids.question: How did God warn Abimelech not to keep Sarah?
kids.option_b: He spoke to him in a dream
kids.option_c: He sent a plague of locusts
kids.explanation: God came to Abimelech in a dream by night (Genesis 20:3).

kids.question: Did Abimelech give Sarah back to Abraham?
kids.explanation: Abimelech restored Sarah to Abraham along with gifts (Genesis 20:14).

teens.question: Why did Abraham once again say Sarah was his sister?
teens.option_b: He feared the people of Gerar would kill him for his wife
teens.option_c: God told him to test Abimelech
teens.option_d: He forgot she was his wife
teens.explanation: Abraham said, "Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place; and they will slay me for my wife's sake" (Genesis 20:11).

teens.question: What did God threaten Abimelech with in the dream?
teens.option_b: Death, because the woman was a man's wife
teens.explanation: God said, "Behold, thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken; for she is a man's wife" (Genesis 20:3).

teens.question: Was Abraham technically lying when he called Sarah his sister?
teens.option_a: Yes, it was a complete lie
teens.option_b: No — Sarah was his half-sister, the daughter of his father but not his mother
teens.option_c: Yes, she was no relation at all
teens.option_d: It was a cultural custom with no truth
teens.explanation: Abraham said, "She is my sister; she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother" (Genesis 20:12).

teens.question: What gifts did Abimelech give Abraham?
teens.option_b: Sheep, oxen, servants, and a thousand pieces of silver
teens.explanation: Abimelech gave Abraham sheep, oxen, menservants, womenservants, and a thousand pieces of silver (Genesis 20:14-16).

teens.question: What did Abraham do for Abimelech after Sarah was returned?
teens.option_b: He prayed to God, and God healed Abimelech and his household
teens.explanation: Abraham prayed unto God: and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants (Genesis 20:17).

adults.question: What had God done to Abimelech's household because of Sarah?
adults.option_b: Closed up all the wombs of his house
adults.explanation: The LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah (Genesis 20:18).

adults.question: How did Abimelech defend his integrity before God?
adults.option_b: He said he acted in the integrity of his heart and innocence of his hands
adults.option_d: He promised to build a temple
adults.explanation: Abimelech said, "In the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this" (Genesis 20:5).

adults.question: How did God respond to Abimelech's claim of innocence?
adults.option_b: God confirmed it, saying He knew and had prevented Abimelech from sinning
adults.explanation: God said, "I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart; for I also withheld thee from sinning against me" (Genesis 20:6).

adults.question: What does God call Abraham in Genesis 20:7?
adults.option_b: A prophet
adults.explanation: God said to Abimelech, "Restore the man his wife; for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee" (Genesis 20:7).

adults.question: What moral tension does this chapter present regarding Abraham?
adults.option_b: Abraham, called a prophet by God, repeated the same deception about Sarah that he used in Egypt
adults.option_c: Abraham was punished for his deception
adults.option_d: Abimelech was more sinful than Abraham
adults.explanation: Abraham repeats the wife-sister deception from Genesis 12, showing even the father of faith struggled with recurring sin.

adults.question: What does Abimelech's rebuke of Abraham in Genesis 20:9-10 reveal?
adults.option_b: Even a pagan king recognized the moral wrongness of Abraham's deception
adults.option_c: Abimelech was a believer in God
adults.option_d: Abraham was justified in his actions
adults.explanation: Abimelech rebuked Abraham: "What hast thou done unto us?...thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done" (Genesis 20:9).

adults.question: What does the thousand pieces of silver serve as according to Abimelech?
adults.option_b: A covering of the eyes — a vindication of Sarah's honor
adults.explanation: Abimelech said the silver is "a covering of the eyes, unto all that are with thee, and with all other" (Genesis 20:16).

adults.question: What does God's protection of Sarah in this chapter demonstrate about the Abrahamic promise?
adults.option_b: God will preserve the covenant line and the promised heir (Isaac) regardless of human failure
adults.option_c: God favors Abraham over all others
adults.option_d: It has no connection to the covenant
adults.explanation: God's intervention protects Sarah — and the yet-unborn Isaac — ensuring the covenant promise is fulfilled despite Abraham's deception (Genesis 20:6).

kids.question: What was the name of Abraham and Sarah's baby boy?
kids.explanation: Abraham called the name of his son Isaac (Genesis 21:3).

kids.question: Why was Sarah so happy when Isaac was born?
kids.option_b: God had given her a baby in her old age, and she laughed with joy
kids.explanation: Sarah said, "God hath made me to laugh, so that all that hear will laugh with me" (Genesis 21:6).

kids.question: What did God provide for Hagar and Ishmael in the desert?
kids.explanation: God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water (Genesis 21:19).

teens.question: How old was Abraham when Isaac was born?
teens.option_d: One hundred and five years old
teens.explanation: Abraham was an hundred years old, when his son Isaac was born unto him (Genesis 21:5).

teens.question: Why did Sarah want Hagar and Ishmael sent away?
teens.option_b: She saw Ishmael mocking, and did not want him to inherit with Isaac
teens.explanation: Sarah saw the son of Hagar mocking, and said, "Cast out this bondwoman and her son: for the son of this bondwoman shall not be heir with my son" (Genesis 21:9-10).

teens.question: How did Abraham feel about sending Ishmael away?
teens.option_b: It was very grievous to him
teens.explanation: The thing was very grievous in Abraham's sight because of his son (Genesis 21:11).

teens.question: What did God promise Abraham about Ishmael?
teens.option_b: God would make him a nation because he was Abraham's seed
teens.explanation: God said, "Also of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed" (Genesis 21:13).

teens.question: What did Abraham and Abimelech do at Beersheba?
teens.option_b: They made a covenant and swore an oath of peace
teens.explanation: Abraham and Abimelech made a covenant at Beersheba (Genesis 21:27, 31).

adults.question: On what day was Isaac circumcised according to God's command?
adults.option_b: The eighth day
adults.explanation: Abraham circumcised his son Isaac being eight days old, as God had commanded him (Genesis 21:4).

adults.question: What does Paul interpret Hagar and Sarah as representing in Galatians 4?
adults.option_b: Two covenants — Hagar represents the old covenant of law, Sarah the new covenant of promise
adults.explanation: Paul uses Hagar (bondage/law) and Sarah (freedom/promise) as an allegory of the two covenants (Galatians 4:22-31; Genesis 21:10).

adults.question: What does the name Beersheba mean?
adults.option_b: Well of the oath (or well of seven)
adults.explanation: Beersheba means "well of the oath" or "well of seven" — Abraham and Abimelech swore there and Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs (Genesis 21:31).

adults.question: What kind of tree did Abraham plant at Beersheba?
adults.option_b: A grove (tamarisk tree)
adults.explanation: Abraham planted a grove (tamarisk tree) in Beersheba, and called there on the name of the LORD (Genesis 21:33).

adults.question: What name of God did Abraham call upon at Beersheba?
adults.option_b: The everlasting God (El Olam)
adults.explanation: Abraham called there on the name of the LORD, the everlasting God (El Olam) (Genesis 21:33).

adults.question: Why did God tell Abraham to listen to Sarah regarding Ishmael?
adults.option_b: Because in Isaac shall thy seed be called — the covenant line must go through Isaac
adults.explanation: God said, "In all that Sarah hath said unto thee, hearken unto her voice; for in Isaac shall thy seed be called" (Genesis 21:12).

adults.question: What dispute with Abimelech prompted the covenant at Beersheba?
adults.option_b: Abimelech's servants had seized Abraham's well
adults.explanation: Abraham reproved Abimelech because of a well of water, which Abimelech's servants had violently taken away (Genesis 21:25).

adults.question: What role did the seven ewe lambs play in the covenant at Beersheba?
adults.option_a: They were a sacrifice to God
adults.option_b: They served as witness that Abraham had dug the well
adults.option_c: They were a gift for Abimelech's family
adults.option_d: They were payment for the land
adults.explanation: Abraham set seven ewe lambs by themselves as a witness that he had dug the well (Genesis 21:28-30).

kids.question: What did God ask Abraham to offer on the mountain?
kids.explanation: God said, "Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac...and offer him for a burnt offering" (Genesis 22:2).

kids.question: What did Abraham find caught in the bushes?
kids.explanation: Abraham looked and behold behind him a ram caught in a thicket by his horns (Genesis 22:13).

kids.question: Did Abraham have to sacrifice Isaac?
kids.option_b: No — God provided a ram instead
kids.option_c: Yes, but Isaac came back to life
kids.explanation: The angel of the LORD stopped Abraham, and God provided a ram in Isaac's place (Genesis 22:11-13).

teens.question: Where did God tell Abraham to take Isaac for the offering?
teens.explanation: God said, "Get thee into the land of Moriah; and offer him there for a burnt offering" (Genesis 22:2).

teens.question: What did Isaac ask his father on the way up the mountain?
teens.option_b: Where is the lamb for the burnt offering?
teens.option_c: Why are we going to the mountain?
teens.explanation: Isaac said, "Behold the fire and the wood: but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?" (Genesis 22:7).

teens.question: What was Abraham's famous answer about the lamb?
teens.option_a: We will find one on the way
teens.option_b: God will provide himself a lamb
teens.explanation: Abraham said, "My son, God will provide himself a lamb for a burnt offering" (Genesis 22:8).

teens.question: What stopped Abraham from sacrificing Isaac?
teens.option_b: The angel of the LORD called from heaven and said "Lay not thine hand upon the lad"
teens.explanation: The angel of the LORD called unto him out of heaven, and said, "Lay not thine hand upon the lad" (Genesis 22:11-12).

teens.question: What name did Abraham give the place?
teens.explanation: Abraham called the name of that place Jehovah-jireh: "In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen" (Genesis 22:14).

adults.question: What Hebrew word is used for what God did to Abraham in Genesis 22:1?
adults.option_b: Tempted (tested/proved)
adults.explanation: God did tempt (Hebrew: nissah — tested/proved) Abraham (Genesis 22:1).

adults.question: How does the binding of Isaac (Aqedah) foreshadow the sacrifice of Christ?
adults.option_a: It does not relate to Christ
adults.option_b: The beloved only son, carrying wood up a mount, willingly submitted, and God provided a substitute — paralleling Christ on the cross
adults.explanation: Isaac as the beloved son carrying wood up Moriah, willingly submitted, and a substitute was provided — all foreshadow Christ (Genesis 22:2, 6, 13).

adults.question: What does Hebrews 11:17-19 say about Abraham's mindset during this test?
adults.option_b: Abraham accounted that God was able to raise Isaac from the dead
adults.option_d: Abraham planned to find a substitute
adults.explanation: By faith Abraham offered up Isaac, accounting that God was able to raise him up, even from the dead (Hebrews 11:17-19).

adults.question: What is the significance of Mount Moriah in later biblical history?
adults.option_b: It is traditionally identified as the site where Solomon built the temple
adults.option_c: It is where Moses received the Law
adults.option_d: It is where David was anointed
adults.explanation: Mount Moriah is identified with the temple mount in Jerusalem (Genesis 22:2; cf. 2 Chronicles 3:1).

adults.question: What new promise did God add after Abraham passed the test?
adults.option_b: His seed would possess the gate of his enemies, and in his seed all nations would be blessed
adults.explanation: God said, "Thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies; and in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed" (Genesis 22:17-18).

adults.question: What reason did God give for why He now blessed Abraham?
adults.option_b: Because thou hast obeyed my voice and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son
adults.option_d: Because Abraham was the oldest patriarch
adults.explanation: God said, "Because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son" (Genesis 22:16).

adults.question: What prophetic significance does Abraham's statement "God will provide himself a lamb" carry?
adults.option_a: It refers only to the ram in the thicket
adults.option_b: It ultimately points to Christ as the Lamb of God whom God Himself provides
adults.option_c: It was an empty reassurance to Isaac
adults.option_d: It refers to the Passover lamb only
adults.explanation: "God will provide himself a lamb" (Genesis 22:8) finds ultimate fulfillment in John 1:29: "Behold the Lamb of God."

adults.question: How does Genesis 22:12 describe what God now knows about Abraham?
adults.option_b: That Abraham fears God and would not withhold his only son
adults.option_d: That Abraham is the father of many nations
adults.explanation: God said, "Now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only son from me" (Genesis 22:12).

kids.question: Who died in this chapter?
kids.explanation: Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old...and Sarah died (Genesis 23:1-2).

kids.question: How old was Sarah when she died?
kids.option_b: One hundred and twenty-seven years old
kids.option_d: One hundred and fifty years old
kids.explanation: Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old (Genesis 23:1).

kids.question: What did Abraham buy to bury Sarah?
kids.explanation: Abraham buried Sarah in the cave of the field of Machpelah (Genesis 23:19).

teens.question: Where did Sarah die?
teens.explanation: Sarah died in Kirjath-arba; the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan (Genesis 23:2).

teens.question: Who owned the cave of Machpelah before Abraham bought it?
teens.explanation: Abraham spoke to Ephron the Hittite to buy the cave of Machpelah (Genesis 23:8-9).

teens.question: How much did Abraham pay for the field and cave?
teens.option_d: He received it as a gift
teens.explanation: Abraham weighed to Ephron four hundred shekels of silver (Genesis 23:16).

teens.question: What did the children of Heth initially offer Abraham?
teens.option_a: To sell him any land he wanted
teens.option_b: To let him bury Sarah in any of their sepulchres
teens.option_c: To build him a new tomb
teens.explanation: The children of Heth said, "In the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead" (Genesis 23:6).

teens.question: Why was it important that Abraham purchased the land rather than accepting a gift?
teens.option_b: He wanted undisputed legal ownership of the burial site in the Promised Land
teens.option_d: He wanted to show off his wealth
teens.explanation: Abraham insisted on paying so he would have legal, permanent ownership of land in Canaan (Genesis 23:13).

adults.question: What does Abraham call himself among the Hittites?
adults.option_b: A stranger and a sojourner
adults.explanation: Abraham said, "I am a stranger and a sojourner with you" (Genesis 23:4).

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Machpelah as the first real estate purchase in the Promised Land?
adults.option_a: It was merely a practical need
adults.option_b: It represents the first tangible stake in the Promised Land, a down payment on God's promise of the whole land
adults.explanation: Machpelah is the first piece of the Promised Land Abraham actually owned, a concrete step toward the fulfillment of God's land promise.

adults.question: Who else was eventually buried at Machpelah besides Sarah?
adults.option_b: Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah
adults.explanation: Machpelah became the family burial site for Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah (Genesis 49:29-31; 50:13).

adults.question: What ancient Near Eastern custom is reflected in the negotiation between Abraham and Ephron?
adults.option_b: The public offer of a "gift" with the expectation of payment — a polite bargaining protocol
adults.option_c: A trade of goods for goods
adults.option_d: A legal contract written on parchment
adults.explanation: Ephron's offer to give the field for free was the ancient Near Eastern protocol — the expectation was that Abraham would insist on paying (Genesis 23:11-16).

adults.question: Where in Canaan is Hebron located?
adults.option_b: In the southern hill country of Judah
adults.explanation: Hebron (Kirjath-arba) is located in the southern hill country of Judah (Genesis 23:2).

adults.question: What phrase indicates that the transaction was legally binding and public?
adults.option_b: Abraham weighed the silver "current money with the merchant" in the audience of the sons of Heth
adults.option_d: They called for witnesses from another city
adults.explanation: Abraham weighed four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant, in the audience of the sons of Heth (Genesis 23:16).

adults.question: What items were included in the purchase beyond the cave itself?
adults.option_b: The field, the cave, and all the trees in the field
adults.option_d: The cave, a well, and livestock
adults.explanation: The field, and the cave therein, and all the trees that were in the field...were made sure unto Abraham (Genesis 23:17).

adults.question: What does the phrase "children of Heth" refer to?
adults.option_b: The Hittites, descendants of Heth son of Canaan
adults.explanation: The children of Heth are the Hittites, descended from Heth, a son of Canaan (Genesis 23:3; cf. 10:15).

kids.question: Who did Abraham send his servant to find?
kids.option_a: A new land to live in
kids.option_b: A wife for his son Isaac
kids.option_d: A place to build a temple
kids.explanation: Abraham told his servant to go to his kindred and take a wife for Isaac (Genesis 24:4).

kids.question: What was the name of the woman the servant found at the well?
kids.explanation: The damsel's name was Rebekah (Genesis 24:15).

kids.question: What kind thing did Rebekah do for the servant?
kids.option_b: She gave him water and watered his camels too
kids.option_d: She invited him to a party
kids.explanation: Rebekah gave him drink and said, "I will draw water for thy camels also" (Genesis 24:18-19).

teens.question: What oath did Abraham make his servant swear?
teens.option_b: Not to take a wife for Isaac from the Canaanites, but from Abraham's own family
teens.option_d: To return to Ur of the Chaldees
teens.explanation: Abraham said, "Thou shalt not take a wife unto my son of the daughters of the Canaanites...but thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred" (Genesis 24:3-4).

teens.question: What sign did the servant pray for to identify the right woman?
teens.option_a: She would be the most beautiful
teens.option_b: She would offer water to him and his camels
teens.option_c: She would be wearing a red veil
teens.option_d: She would be standing under a tree
teens.explanation: The servant prayed that the damsel who offered to water his camels would be the one God appointed for Isaac (Genesis 24:14).

teens.question: Who was Rebekah's father?
teens.explanation: Rebekah was the daughter of Bethuel son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor (Genesis 24:15).

teens.question: What gifts did the servant give Rebekah?
teens.option_b: A golden earring and two bracelets
teens.explanation: The man took a golden earring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets for her hands (Genesis 24:22).

teens.question: What was Rebekah's answer when asked if she would go with the servant?
teens.option_d: She asked her father to decide
teens.explanation: They called Rebekah, and said, "Wilt thou go with this man?" And she said, "I will go" (Genesis 24:58).

adults.question: Where did Abraham make his servant place his hand when swearing the oath?
adults.option_b: Under Abraham's thigh
adults.explanation: Abraham said, "Put thy hand under my thigh" — an ancient oath gesture signifying the most solemn vow (Genesis 24:2).

adults.question: What did Abraham insist must NOT happen if the woman was unwilling to come?
adults.option_a: The servant should take Isaac there anyway
adults.option_b: Isaac must not be brought back to Mesopotamia
adults.option_c: The servant should force her to come
adults.option_d: The engagement should be called off permanently
adults.explanation: Abraham said, "Beware thou that thou bring not my son thither again" — Isaac must remain in Canaan (Genesis 24:6, 8).

adults.question: How many camels did the servant take on the journey?
adults.option_b: Five
adults.explanation: The servant took ten camels of the camels of his master and departed (Genesis 24:10).

adults.question: What city did the servant travel to?
adults.option_b: The city of Nahor in Mesopotamia
adults.explanation: The servant went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor (Genesis 24:10).

adults.question: What was Laban's role in the negotiations, and what motivated his hospitality?
adults.option_a: He was indifferent to the servant
adults.option_b: Laban ran to greet the servant after seeing the golden jewelry on Rebekah
adults.option_c: Laban refused to let Rebekah go
adults.explanation: When Laban saw the earring and bracelets, he ran to the man and said, "Come in, thou blessed of the LORD" (Genesis 24:30-31).

adults.question: What blessing did Rebekah's family pronounce upon her as she departed?
adults.option_b: Be thou the mother of thousands of millions, and let thy seed possess the gate of those which hate them
adults.option_d: May God protect you on the journey
adults.explanation: They blessed Rebekah: "Be thou the mother of thousands of millions, and let thy seed possess the gate of those which hate them" (Genesis 24:60).

adults.question: What was Isaac doing when Rebekah first saw him?
adults.option_b: Meditating in the field in the evening
adults.explanation: Isaac went out to meditate in the field at the eventide (Genesis 24:63).

adults.question: What does Genesis 24:67 say about Isaac's response to Rebekah?
adults.option_b: Isaac loved her, and was comforted after his mother's death
adults.explanation: Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah's tent, and took Rebekah, and she became his wife; and he loved her: and Isaac was comforted after his mother's death (Genesis 24:67).

kids.question: What were the names of Isaac and Rebekah's twin boys?
kids.explanation: The first came out red and hairy, they called him Esau; and his brother they called Jacob (Genesis 25:25-26).

kids.question: What did Esau sell to Jacob?
kids.explanation: Esau sold his birthright unto Jacob (Genesis 25:33).

kids.question: What food did Jacob give Esau in exchange?
kids.explanation: Jacob gave Esau bread and pottage of lentiles (Genesis 25:34).

teens.question: How old was Abraham when he died?
teens.option_a: One hundred and fifty years old
teens.option_b: One hundred and seventy-five years old
teens.option_d: One hundred and fifty-seven years old
teens.explanation: Abraham gave up the ghost and died in a good old age...an hundred threescore and fifteen years (Genesis 25:7-8).

teens.question: Where was Abraham buried?
teens.explanation: His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah (Genesis 25:9).

teens.question: What did God tell Rebekah about the twins before they were born?
teens.option_b: Two nations were in her womb, and the elder would serve the younger
teens.option_d: The firstborn would be a priest
teens.explanation: The LORD said, "Two nations are in thy womb...and the elder shall serve the younger" (Genesis 25:23).

teens.question: What was Esau like compared to Jacob?
teens.option_b: Esau was a skillful hunter and outdoorsman; Jacob was a quiet man dwelling in tents
teens.option_d: Esau was a farmer and Jacob was a shepherd
teens.explanation: Esau was a cunning hunter, a man of the field; and Jacob was a plain man, dwelling in tents (Genesis 25:27).

teens.question: Why did Esau sell his birthright?
teens.option_b: He was extremely hungry and despised his birthright
teens.explanation: Esau said, "I am at the point to die: and what profit shall this birthright do to me?" He despised his birthright (Genesis 25:32, 34).

adults.question: Who was Keturah, and what is her significance?
adults.option_b: She was Abraham's wife after Sarah's death, and bore him six sons
adults.explanation: Abraham took a wife, and her name was Keturah, and she bare him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah (Genesis 25:1-2).

adults.question: How many sons did Ishmael have, fulfilling God's promise?
adults.option_b: Ten
adults.explanation: Ishmael had twelve sons — twelve princes according to their nations, fulfilling Genesis 17:20 (Genesis 25:13-16).

adults.question: How old was Ishmael when he died?
adults.option_b: One hundred and thirty-seven years
adults.explanation: These are the years of the life of Ishmael, an hundred and thirty and seven years (Genesis 25:17).

adults.question: What does the name Jacob mean, and why was he so named?
adults.option_a: Faithful one — because he was faithful
adults.option_b: Supplanter (heel-catcher) — because he took hold of Esau's heel at birth
adults.option_c: Beloved — because his mother loved him
adults.explanation: His name was called Jacob because his hand took hold on Esau's heel (Genesis 25:26). Jacob means "heel-catcher" or "supplanter."

adults.question: What does Romans 9:10-13 teach about God's statement regarding Jacob and Esau?
adults.option_b: God's election was made before the twins were born or had done good or evil, demonstrating sovereign choice
adults.option_c: God chose based on their future deeds
adults.option_d: It only applied to the nations, not the individuals
adults.explanation: Paul cites "the elder shall serve the younger" to illustrate God's sovereign election before birth (Romans 9:10-13; Genesis 25:23).

adults.question: What does Hebrews 12:16 call Esau for selling his birthright?
adults.option_b: A profane person who for one morsel of meat sold his birthright
adults.explanation: Hebrews 12:16 calls Esau a "profane person, who for one morsel of meat sold his birthright."

adults.question: What did Abraham give to Isaac versus the sons of Keturah and the concubines?
adults.option_b: Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac, but gave gifts to the other sons and sent them away
adults.option_c: He gave more to the sons of Keturah
adults.explanation: Abraham gave all that he had unto Isaac. But unto the sons of the concubines, Abraham gave gifts, and sent them away (Genesis 25:5-6).

adults.question: How old was Isaac when Esau and Jacob were born?
adults.option_b: Fifty years old
adults.explanation: Isaac was threescore years old when she bare them (Genesis 25:26).

kids.question: Who told people that Rebekah was his sister instead of his wife?
kids.explanation: Isaac said Rebekah was his sister because he was afraid. See Genesis 26:7.

kids.question: What did Isaac dig in the land?
kids.explanation: Isaac's servants dug wells in the valley. See Genesis 26:18-19.

kids.question: What was the name of the king Isaac dealt with?
kids.explanation: Abimelech was king of the Philistines. See Genesis 26:1.

teens.question: Why did Isaac go to Gerar instead of Egypt during the famine?
teens.option_a: He was too old to travel far
teens.option_d: God told him not to go to Egypt
teens.explanation: The LORD appeared to Isaac and said "Go not down into Egypt." See Genesis 26:2.

teens.question: What were the names of the first two wells that caused strife?
teens.explanation: They called them Esek (contention) and Sitnah (hatred). See Genesis 26:20-21.

teens.question: What does the name Rehoboth mean?
teens.option_d: The LORD hath made room for us
teens.explanation: Isaac said "the LORD hath made room for us." See Genesis 26:22.

teens.question: Why did Abimelech come to Isaac at Beersheba?
teens.option_a: To make a covenant of peace
teens.explanation: Abimelech sought a covenant because he saw the LORD was with Isaac. See Genesis 26:28.

teens.question: What grief did Esau's wives cause Isaac and Rebekah?
teens.option_c: They were a grief of mind because they were Hittite women
teens.option_d: They refused to help the family
teens.explanation: Esau's Hittite wives were a grief of mind to Isaac and Rebekah. See Genesis 26:34-35.

adults.question: God's promise to Isaac in Genesis 26:3-4 echoes which earlier covenant?
adults.option_b: The covenant with Melchizedek
adults.explanation: God reaffirmed the Abrahamic covenant: multiplied seed, all nations blessed. See Genesis 26:3-4.

adults.question: What reason did God give for confirming His promises to Isaac?
adults.option_b: Because of Rebekah's faith
adults.option_c: Because Abraham obeyed God's voice and kept His commandments
adults.explanation: God said "Because that Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge." See Genesis 26:5.

adults.question: How did Abimelech discover that Rebekah was Isaac's wife?
adults.option_b: He saw Isaac sporting with Rebekah
adults.explanation: Abimelech looked out a window and saw Isaac sporting with Rebekah. See Genesis 26:8.

adults.question: How successful was Isaac's farming in Gerar?
adults.option_a: He received a hundredfold in the same year
adults.option_b: He barely survived
adults.explanation: Isaac sowed and received a hundredfold, and the LORD blessed him. See Genesis 26:12.

adults.question: What did the Philistines do to Abraham's wells after his death?
adults.option_b: They built walls around them
adults.explanation: The Philistines had stopped and filled Abraham's wells with earth. See Genesis 26:15.

adults.question: Who accompanied Abimelech when he came to make a covenant with Isaac?
adults.option_a: Ahuzzath his friend and Phichol the chief captain
adults.option_b: His army commander only
adults.explanation: Abimelech came with Ahuzzath his friend and Phichol the chief captain. See Genesis 26:26.

adults.question: What did Isaac's servants find on the same day Abimelech departed?
adults.option_b: An ancient altar
adults.explanation: Isaac's servants told him about a well they had digged and found water. See Genesis 26:32.

adults.question: What were the names of Esau's two Hittite wives mentioned in this chapter?
adults.option_b: Judith and Bashemath
adults.explanation: Esau married Judith daughter of Beeri and Bashemath daughter of Elon. See Genesis 26:34.

kids.question: Who helped Jacob trick his father Isaac?
kids.explanation: Rebekah told Jacob how to disguise himself and get the blessing. See Genesis 27:6-10.

kids.question: What did Jacob put on his hands and neck to feel hairy like Esau?
kids.explanation: Rebekah put goat skins on Jacob's hands and neck. See Genesis 27:16.

kids.question: What did Esau do when he found out Jacob took his blessing?
kids.option_c: He cried with a great and bitter cry
kids.explanation: Esau cried with a great and exceeding bitter cry. See Genesis 27:34.

teens.question: Why was Isaac unable to see who was really before him?
teens.option_a: It was dark in the tent
teens.option_d: His eyes were dim with age
teens.explanation: Isaac was old and his eyes were dim so he could not see. See Genesis 27:1.

teens.question: What food did Isaac ask Esau to prepare before giving the blessing?
teens.explanation: Isaac asked Esau to hunt venison and make savoury meat. See Genesis 27:3-4.

teens.question: What did Isaac notice about the voice versus the hands?
teens.option_d: The voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau
teens.explanation: Isaac noted the voice was Jacob's but the hands were Esau's. See Genesis 27:22.

teens.question: What blessing did Isaac give to Esau after Jacob took the first?
teens.option_a: He would live by his sword and serve his brother but eventually break free
teens.option_b: He would be a great king
teens.option_c: He would inherit the promised land
teens.option_d: He would have more children than Jacob
teens.explanation: Isaac said Esau would live by his sword and serve his brother but break his yoke. See Genesis 27:39-40.

teens.question: Where did Rebekah tell Jacob to flee to escape Esau's anger?
teens.explanation: Rebekah told Jacob to flee to Laban her brother in Haran. See Genesis 27:43.

adults.question: In Isaac's blessing of Jacob, what specific blessings were promised?
adults.option_b: Olive groves and vineyards
adults.option_d: The dew of heaven and the fatness of the earth, plenty of corn and wine
adults.explanation: Isaac blessed Jacob with dew of heaven, fatness of earth, corn and wine. See Genesis 27:28.

adults.question: What political authority did Isaac's blessing confer on Jacob?
adults.option_b: Priesthood
adults.option_c: Lordship over his brethren, with nations bowing down
adults.explanation: Isaac said "Be lord over thy brethren, let thy mother's sons bow down to thee." See Genesis 27:29.

adults.question: How did Jacob describe himself to his mother when she proposed the deception?
adults.option_b: A smooth man, unlike Esau who was hairy
adults.explanation: Jacob said "Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man." See Genesis 27:11.

adults.question: What curse did Rebekah say she would bear if the deception failed?
adults.option_a: Upon me be thy curse, my son
adults.option_b: Banishment from the family
adults.explanation: Rebekah said "Upon me be thy curse, my son." See Genesis 27:13.

adults.question: When Isaac discovered the deception, what did he say about the blessing?
adults.option_b: He split it between both sons
adults.option_c: I have blessed him, yea and he shall be blessed
adults.explanation: Isaac confirmed the blessing could not be revoked. See Genesis 27:33.

adults.question: What did Esau resolve to do after losing his blessing?
adults.option_a: Kill Jacob after the days of mourning for Isaac
adults.option_b: Seek his own blessing elsewhere
adults.explanation: Esau said he would slay his brother after their father's death. See Genesis 27:41.

adults.question: What excuse did Rebekah give Isaac for sending Jacob away?
adults.option_a: She feared Esau would kill him
adults.option_b: She had a dream from God
adults.option_c: She wanted Jacob to study with Laban
adults.option_d: She was weary of life because of Heth's daughters and did not want Jacob to marry one
adults.explanation: Rebekah told Isaac she was weary because of Heth's daughters. See Genesis 27:46.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Isaac's inability to revoke the blessing?
adults.option_a: Isaac was too weak to argue
adults.option_b: It reflects the irrevocable nature of patriarchal blessings spoken under divine authority
adults.option_c: He simply forgot what he said
adults.explanation: The patriarchal blessing was considered divinely binding. See Genesis 27:33 and Hebrews 12:17.

kids.question: What did Jacob see in his dream at Bethel?
kids.option_a: A ladder reaching to heaven with angels going up and down
kids.explanation: Jacob dreamed of a ladder with angels ascending and descending. See Genesis 28:12.

kids.question: What did Jacob use as a pillow when he slept?
kids.explanation: Jacob took stones and put them for his pillows. See Genesis 28:11.

kids.question: What did Jacob name the place where he had the dream?
kids.explanation: Jacob called the place Bethel, meaning house of God. See Genesis 28:19.

teens.question: Why did Isaac send Jacob to Padanaram?
teens.option_d: To find a wife from Laban's family rather than from the Canaanites
teens.explanation: Isaac charged Jacob not to take a Canaanite wife but to go to Laban's house. See Genesis 28:1-2.

teens.question: What was the original name of Bethel before Jacob renamed it?
teens.explanation: The name of the city was called Luz at the first. See Genesis 28:19.

teens.question: What did God promise Jacob in the dream?
teens.option_d: The land, countless descendants, and that all families of earth would be blessed through him
teens.explanation: God promised the land, seed as the dust, and blessing to all families. See Genesis 28:13-14.

teens.question: What did Jacob do with the stone pillow in the morning?
teens.option_a: Set it up as a pillar and poured oil on it
teens.explanation: Jacob set up the stone as a pillar and poured oil on the top of it. See Genesis 28:18.

teens.question: What vow did Jacob make to God at Bethel?
teens.option_c: If God would be with him and provide, the LORD would be his God and he would give a tenth
teens.explanation: Jacob vowed that God would be his God and he would give a tenth. See Genesis 28:20-22.

adults.question: What did Esau do when he saw Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him to Padanaram?
adults.option_b: He made peace with Jacob
adults.option_d: He went to Ishmael and married Mahalath, Ishmael's daughter
adults.explanation: Esau went to Ishmael and took Mahalath as wife. See Genesis 28:8-9.

adults.question: In Jacob's dream, where did the LORD stand?
adults.option_a: At the bottom of the ladder
adults.option_b: Beside Jacob
adults.explanation: The LORD stood above the ladder and spoke to Jacob. See Genesis 28:13.

adults.question: What specific directional expansion did God promise Jacob's descendants?
adults.option_b: To the west, east, north, and south
adults.explanation: God said thou shalt spread abroad to the west, east, north, and south. See Genesis 28:14.

adults.question: What was Jacob's first reaction upon waking from the dream?
adults.option_a: He was afraid and said how dreadful is this place
adults.option_b: Joy
adults.explanation: Jacob was afraid and said "How dreadful is this place!" See Genesis 28:17.

adults.question: What did Jacob call Bethel besides house of God?
adults.option_b: God's dwelling
adults.explanation: Jacob said this is none other but the house of God and the gate of heaven. See Genesis 28:17.

adults.question: Jacob's vow in 28:20-22 is conditional. What conditions did he set?
adults.option_a: If God will be with him, keep him, give him bread and raiment, and bring him back in peace
adults.option_b: If God makes him wealthy and powerful
adults.option_d: If God gives him many sons
adults.explanation: Jacob asked for God's presence, protection, provision, and safe return. See Genesis 28:20-21.

adults.question: Who was Mahalath's father, the wife Esau took from Ishmael's family?
adults.option_b: Nahor
adults.explanation: Esau married Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael. See Genesis 28:9.

adults.question: How does Jacob's Bethel experience connect to Jesus' words in John 1:51?
adults.option_a: Jesus mentions a pillar of stone
adults.option_b: Jesus tells Nathanael he will see heaven open and angels ascending and descending upon the Son of man
adults.option_d: Jesus rebukes Nathanael using Jacob's story
adults.explanation: Jesus identified Himself as the true ladder between heaven and earth. See John 1:51 and Genesis 28:12.

kids.question: Who did Jacob meet at the well?
kids.explanation: Jacob met Rachel at the well as she came with her father's sheep. See Genesis 29:9-10.

kids.question: How many years did Jacob agree to work for Laban to marry Rachel?
kids.explanation: Jacob served seven years for Rachel. See Genesis 29:18.

kids.question: Who did Laban trick Jacob into marrying first?
kids.explanation: Laban gave Leah to Jacob instead of Rachel. See Genesis 29:23-25.

teens.question: What was Rachel's job when Jacob first saw her?
teens.option_b: She was selling goods at market
teens.option_d: She was a shepherdess tending her father's sheep
teens.explanation: Rachel came with her father's sheep, for she kept them. See Genesis 29:9.

teens.question: What did Jacob do when he first met Rachel at the well?
teens.option_b: He rolled the stone from the well's mouth, watered her flock, kissed her, and wept
teens.option_d: He asked permission to speak to her
teens.explanation: Jacob rolled the stone, watered the flock, kissed Rachel, and wept. See Genesis 29:10-11.

teens.question: How did the seven years Jacob worked for Rachel seem to him?
teens.option_d: Like only a few days because of his love for her
teens.explanation: They seemed unto him but a few days, for the love he had to her. See Genesis 29:20.

teens.question: What excuse did Laban give for substituting Leah for Rachel?
teens.option_a: It was the custom that the firstborn must marry before the younger
teens.option_d: He had promised Leah to Jacob already
teens.explanation: Laban said it was not done in their country to give the younger before the firstborn. See Genesis 29:26.

teens.question: What does the name Reuben relate to?
teens.option_c: The LORD had looked upon Leah's affliction
teens.explanation: Leah said "the LORD hath looked upon my affliction." See Genesis 29:32.

adults.question: How is Leah physically described in contrast to Rachel?
adults.option_a: Leah was short, Rachel was tall
adults.option_b: No physical differences are mentioned
adults.option_c: Leah was dark-haired, Rachel was fair
adults.option_d: Leah was tender eyed; Rachel was beautiful and well favoured
adults.explanation: Leah was tender eyed but Rachel was beautiful and well favoured. See Genesis 29:17.

adults.question: How many sons did Leah bear to Jacob in this chapter?
adults.option_b: Three
adults.explanation: Leah bore Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah in this chapter. See Genesis 29:32-35.

adults.question: What is the meaning of the name Simeon?
adults.option_b: The LORD heard that I was hated
adults.explanation: Leah said "Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated." See Genesis 29:33.

adults.question: What did Leah say when she named her son Levi?
adults.option_a: Now this time will my husband be joined unto me
adults.option_b: The LORD has heard me
adults.option_c: Now will I praise the LORD
adults.explanation: Leah hoped her husband would be joined to her. See Genesis 29:34.

adults.question: After Judah was born, what did Leah say?
adults.option_a: Now my husband will love me
adults.option_b: God has given me another son
adults.option_c: Now will I praise the LORD
adults.explanation: Leah said "Now will I praise the LORD" and called him Judah. See Genesis 29:35.

adults.question: What is the relationship between Laban and Rebekah?
adults.option_b: They were cousins
adults.explanation: Laban was Rebekah's brother. See Genesis 29:5 and Genesis 24:29.

adults.question: What was Jacob's total labor commitment to earn Rachel as his wife?
adults.option_b: Ten years
adults.option_c: Seven years plus a bride price
adults.explanation: Jacob served seven years, was tricked, then served another seven. See Genesis 29:27-30.

adults.question: What theological pattern does God's opening of Leah's womb while Rachel was barren establish?
adults.option_b: God shows compassion to the unloved and works through unexpected means
adults.explanation: When the LORD saw Leah was hated, he opened her womb. See Genesis 29:31.

kids.question: What did Rachel ask Jacob to give her?
kids.explanation: Rachel said to Jacob "Give me children, or else I die." See Genesis 30:1.

kids.question: What kind of animals did Jacob ask for as his wages from Laban?
kids.explanation: Jacob asked for the speckled and spotted cattle and goats. See Genesis 30:32.

kids.question: What was the name of Rachel's first son?
kids.explanation: God remembered Rachel and she bore a son named Joseph. See Genesis 30:22-24.

teens.question: Which handmaid did Rachel give to Jacob to bear children on her behalf?
teens.explanation: Rachel gave Jacob Bilhah her handmaid. See Genesis 30:3-4.

teens.question: Which handmaid did Leah give to Jacob?
teens.explanation: Leah gave Jacob Zilpah her maid. See Genesis 30:9.

teens.question: What plant did Reuben find in the field that Rachel wanted?
teens.explanation: Reuben found mandrakes in the field. See Genesis 30:14.

teens.question: What was the name of the only daughter of Jacob mentioned in Genesis?
teens.explanation: Leah bore a daughter and called her name Dinah. See Genesis 30:21.

teens.question: What did Laban realize about why he had prospered?
teens.option_c: The LORD had blessed him for Jacob's sake
teens.explanation: Laban said "I have learned by experience that the LORD hath blessed me for thy sake." See Genesis 30:27.

adults.question: What does the name Dan mean, as given by Rachel?
adults.option_b: He is mighty
adults.option_d: God hath judged me and heard my voice
adults.explanation: Rachel said "God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice." See Genesis 30:6.

adults.question: What does the name Naphtali signify?
adults.option_b: Fruitful vine
adults.option_c: With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister
adults.explanation: Rachel said "With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister." See Genesis 30:8.

adults.question: What does the name Issachar relate to?
adults.option_b: God hath given me my hire because I gave my maiden to my husband
adults.explanation: Leah said "God hath given me my hire." See Genesis 30:18.

adults.question: What does the name Zebulun mean according to Leah?
adults.option_a: God hath endued me with a good dowry; now will my husband dwell with me
adults.option_b: God has blessed me
adults.explanation: Leah said "now will my husband dwell with me." See Genesis 30:20.

adults.question: What does the name Gad mean?
adults.option_b: Great warrior
adults.explanation: Leah said "A troop cometh" and called his name Gad. See Genesis 30:11.

adults.question: What strategy did Jacob use with rods of poplar, hazel, and chestnut?
adults.option_a: He peeled white strakes in them and set them before the flocks at watering troughs
adults.option_b: He built fences
adults.option_c: He used them as walking sticks
adults.explanation: Jacob placed peeled rods in the gutters before the breeding flocks. See Genesis 30:37-39.

adults.question: How did Jacob selectively breed the stronger animals?
adults.option_b: He kept them in separate pastures
adults.option_d: He set the rods before the stronger cattle but not the feebler ones
adults.explanation: Jacob laid the rods before the stronger cattle and not the feebler. See Genesis 30:41-42.

adults.question: What was the result of Jacob's livestock strategy?
adults.option_b: He increased exceedingly with large flocks, maidservants, menservants, camels, and asses
adults.option_d: He lost most of his flock
adults.explanation: The man increased exceedingly and had much cattle. See Genesis 30:43.

kids.question: Why did Jacob decide to leave Laban?
kids.option_a: Laban and his sons were no longer friendly toward him
kids.explanation: Jacob saw that Laban's countenance was not toward him as before. See Genesis 31:2.

kids.question: What did Rachel steal from her father Laban?
kids.explanation: Rachel had stolen the images (teraphim) that were her father's. See Genesis 31:19.

kids.question: Did Laban find his stolen household gods?
kids.option_a: Yes, he found them right away
kids.option_c: No, Rachel hid them and he did not find them
kids.explanation: Rachel sat on the images and Laban searched but found them not. See Genesis 31:34-35.

teens.question: How did God tell Jacob to leave Laban?
teens.option_c: Through an angel in a dream
teens.option_d: God spoke to Jacob and said return unto the land of thy fathers
teens.explanation: The LORD said unto Jacob "Return unto the land of thy fathers." See Genesis 31:3.

teens.question: How many times did Laban change Jacob's wages according to Jacob?
teens.explanation: Jacob said Laban had changed his wages ten times. See Genesis 31:7.

teens.question: How did Rachel hide the household gods from Laban?
teens.option_a: She buried them in the ground
teens.option_b: She gave them to a servant
teens.option_c: She hid them in a well
teens.option_d: She sat upon them and said she could not rise because of her monthly period
teens.explanation: Rachel sat upon the camel's furniture with the images inside and said she could not rise. See Genesis 31:34-35.

teens.question: What covenant did Laban and Jacob make?
teens.option_a: A covenant at Mizpah with a heap of stones as witness
teens.explanation: They made a covenant at Mizpah with a pillar and heap. See Genesis 31:44-49.

teens.question: What does the name Mizpah mean in the covenant context?
teens.option_c: The LORD watch between me and thee when we are absent one from another
teens.explanation: Laban said "The LORD watch between me and thee." See Genesis 31:49.

adults.question: How long did Jacob serve Laban in total?
adults.option_b: Twenty-five years
adults.explanation: Jacob served fourteen years for the two daughters and six years for the cattle. See Genesis 31:41.

adults.question: What did God say to Laban in a dream before he caught up with Jacob?
adults.option_b: Forgive Jacob freely
adults.option_c: Take heed that thou speak not to Jacob either good or bad
adults.explanation: God warned Laban not to speak to Jacob either good or bad. See Genesis 31:24.

adults.question: How did Jacob describe his service to Laban?
adults.option_b: In the day drought consumed him and frost by night, and sleep departed from his eyes
adults.explanation: Jacob described harsh conditions of drought, frost, and sleeplessness. See Genesis 31:40.

adults.question: What did Jacob say would have happened if God had not been on his side?
adults.option_a: Laban would have sent him away empty
adults.option_b: He would have stayed longer
adults.option_c: He would have become wealthy sooner
adults.explanation: Jacob said Laban would have sent him away empty except God intervened. See Genesis 31:42.

adults.question: What names did Laban and Jacob give to the heap of witness?
adults.option_b: Peniel and Mahanaim
adults.explanation: Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha (Aramaic) and Jacob called it Galeed (Hebrew). See Genesis 31:47.

adults.question: What angel did Jacob reference when explaining how God showed him to breed the cattle?
adults.option_a: The angel of God who spoke in a dream
adults.option_b: Michael
adults.option_d: The angel of the LORD at the bush
adults.explanation: The angel of God spoke to Jacob in a dream about the cattle. See Genesis 31:11-12.

adults.question: What specific charge did Laban make the covenant cover regarding Jacob's treatment of his daughters?
adults.option_a: That Jacob would not make them work
adults.option_b: That Jacob would give them an inheritance
adults.option_c: That Jacob would send them home yearly
adults.option_d: That Jacob would not afflict his daughters nor take other wives besides them
adults.explanation: Laban said "If thou shalt afflict my daughters, or if thou shalt take other wives." See Genesis 31:50.

adults.question: By whose God did Jacob swear at the Mizpah covenant?
adults.option_b: The fear of his father Isaac
adults.explanation: Jacob sware by the fear of his father Isaac. See Genesis 31:53.

kids.question: Who did Jacob wrestle with all night?
kids.explanation: Jacob wrestled with a man until the breaking of day. See Genesis 32:24.

kids.question: What new name did Jacob receive after the wrestling?
kids.explanation: His name was changed to Israel. See Genesis 32:28.

kids.question: What did Jacob send ahead as gifts to Esau?
kids.option_c: Goats, sheep, camels, cattle, and donkeys
kids.explanation: Jacob sent goats, sheep, camels, cows, bulls, and donkeys. See Genesis 32:14-15.

teens.question: What river did Jacob cross the night he wrestled with the angel?
teens.explanation: Jacob was left alone after crossing the ford of Jabbok. See Genesis 32:22-24.

teens.question: How many men was Esau coming with when Jacob heard the news?
teens.explanation: Esau was coming with four hundred men. See Genesis 32:6.

teens.question: What did the angel do to Jacob during the wrestling match?
teens.option_d: Touched the hollow of his thigh, putting it out of joint
teens.explanation: He touched the hollow of Jacob's thigh and it was out of joint. See Genesis 32:25.

teens.question: What did Jacob demand before letting the angel go?
teens.explanation: Jacob said "I will not let thee go, except thou bless me." See Genesis 32:26.

teens.question: What does the name Israel mean?
teens.option_c: A prince who has power with God and with men, and has prevailed
teens.explanation: He said "thou hast power with God and with men, and hast prevailed." See Genesis 32:28.

adults.question: What did Jacob name the place where he met angels of God at the beginning of the chapter?
adults.option_b: Succoth
adults.explanation: Jacob called the place Mahanaim, meaning two hosts or two camps. See Genesis 32:2.

adults.question: What strategy did Jacob use to protect his family from Esau?
adults.option_b: He hired soldiers
adults.option_c: He divided his people and flocks into two bands so if one was attacked the other could escape
adults.explanation: Jacob divided his company into two bands for safety. See Genesis 32:7-8.

adults.question: In Jacob's prayer before meeting Esau, what covenant promise did he invoke?
adults.option_b: God's promise to do him good and make his seed as the sand of the sea
adults.explanation: Jacob reminded God of the promise to make his seed as the sand. See Genesis 32:12.

adults.question: What did Jacob call the place where he wrestled with God?
adults.option_a: Peniel, for I have seen God face to face and my life is preserved
adults.option_b: Bethel
adults.explanation: Jacob called it Peniel: "I have seen God face to face." See Genesis 32:30.

adults.question: Why do the children of Israel not eat the sinew of the thigh?
adults.option_b: Moses commanded it later
adults.option_c: Because the angel touched the hollow of Jacob's thigh in the sinew that shrank
adults.explanation: The children of Israel eat not the sinew which shrank because of the angel's touch. See Genesis 32:32.

adults.question: How did Jacob describe himself in his prayer to God?
adults.option_a: As unworthy of the least of all God's mercies and truth
adults.option_b: As a mighty prince
adults.explanation: Jacob said "I am not worthy of the least of all the mercies." See Genesis 32:10.

adults.question: What did Jacob instruct his servants to say when Esau met each drove of gifts?
adults.option_b: Jacob has come to trade
adults.option_d: They be thy servant Jacob's; it is a present sent unto my lord Esau; and behold also he is behind us
adults.explanation: Each servant was to say the gifts were a present for lord Esau. See Genesis 32:18.

adults.question: Why did the angel ask Jacob his name before renaming him?
adults.option_b: To make Jacob confess his identity as the supplanter before receiving the new name Israel
adults.explanation: Confessing his name Jacob (supplanter) preceded receiving the name Israel. See Genesis 32:27-28.

kids.question: What did Esau do when he finally met Jacob?
kids.option_a: He ran to meet him, embraced him, and kissed him
kids.explanation: Esau ran to meet Jacob, embraced him, fell on his neck, and kissed him. See Genesis 33:4.

kids.question: Were Jacob and Esau enemies when they met again?
kids.option_b: No, they wept together and made peace
kids.explanation: They wept together and reconciled. See Genesis 33:4.

kids.question: Where did Jacob settle after parting from Esau?
kids.explanation: Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem, and pitched his tent. See Genesis 33:18.

teens.question: How did Jacob arrange his family when approaching Esau?
teens.option_b: He sent them away to safety
teens.option_d: Handmaids and their children first, Leah and hers next, Rachel and Joseph last
teens.explanation: Jacob put the handmaids first, Leah next, and Rachel and Joseph last. See Genesis 33:2.

teens.question: How many times did Jacob bow to the ground before reaching Esau?
teens.explanation: Jacob bowed himself to the ground seven times. See Genesis 33:3.

teens.question: Did Esau want to accept Jacob's gifts at first?
teens.option_d: No, he said he had enough and Jacob had to urge him
teens.explanation: Esau said "I have enough, my brother; keep that thou hast." See Genesis 33:9.

teens.question: What reason did Jacob give for not traveling with Esau to Seir?
teens.option_a: The children were tender and the flocks with young would be overdriven
teens.option_c: He wanted to go a different direction
teens.explanation: Jacob said the children were tender and the flocks nursing. See Genesis 33:13.

teens.question: What did Jacob build at Succoth?
teens.option_c: A house for himself and booths for his cattle
teens.explanation: Jacob built a house and made booths for his cattle, so the place was called Succoth. See Genesis 33:17.

adults.question: What remarkable comparison did Jacob make when seeing Esau's face?
adults.option_b: Like seeing a brother for the first time
adults.option_d: As though I had seen the face of God, and thou wast pleased with me
adults.explanation: Jacob said seeing Esau's face was like seeing the face of God. See Genesis 33:10.

adults.question: What word did Jacob use when referring to his gifts to Esau?
adults.option_b: My payment
adults.option_c: My blessing that is brought to thee
adults.explanation: Jacob called it "my blessing that is brought to thee." See Genesis 33:11.

adults.question: Where did Esau return to after the reconciliation?
adults.option_b: Seir
adults.explanation: Esau returned that day on his way unto Seir. See Genesis 33:16.

adults.question: What did Jacob buy at Shechem?
adults.option_a: A parcel of a field where he had spread his tent
adults.option_b: A house
adults.explanation: Jacob bought a parcel of field from the children of Hamor. See Genesis 33:19.

adults.question: How much did Jacob pay for the field at Shechem?
adults.option_b: Ten shekels of gold
adults.explanation: He bought it for an hundred pieces of money. See Genesis 33:19.

adults.question: What did Jacob name the altar he built at Shechem?
adults.option_b: El-bethel
adults.explanation: He called the altar El-elohe-Israel. See Genesis 33:20.

adults.question: Jacob's sevenfold bowing before Esau reflects what ancient custom?
adults.option_b: An Egyptian custom
adults.option_d: A standard Near Eastern gesture of submission found in Amarna letters
adults.explanation: Sevenfold bowing was a known Near Eastern diplomatic gesture. See Genesis 33:3.

adults.question: How does Jacob's reconciliation with Esau parallel a larger biblical theme?
adults.option_b: Grace, forgiveness, and reconciliation between estranged brothers
adults.explanation: The reunion illustrates grace and forgiveness despite past wrongs. See Genesis 33:4, 10-11.

kids.question: Who was Dinah?
kids.explanation: Dinah was the daughter of Leah and Jacob. See Genesis 34:1.

kids.question: Which two brothers took revenge for what happened to Dinah?
kids.explanation: Simeon and Levi took swords and attacked the city. See Genesis 34:25.

kids.question: Was Jacob happy about what Simeon and Levi did?
kids.option_c: No, he was troubled and said they had made him to stink among the people of the land
kids.explanation: Jacob said "Ye have troubled me to make me to stink among the inhabitants." See Genesis 34:30.

teens.question: Who was Shechem and what did he do?
teens.option_c: A merchant who traded with Jacob
teens.option_d: The son of Hamor the prince who defiled Dinah
teens.explanation: Shechem the son of Hamor the Hivite defiled Dinah. See Genesis 34:2.

teens.question: What condition did Jacob's sons set before agreeing to let Shechem marry Dinah?
teens.option_b: Every male in Shechem must be circumcised
teens.option_c: Shechem must serve Jacob for seven years
teens.explanation: They required every male to be circumcised. See Genesis 34:15.

teens.question: When did Simeon and Levi attack the city?
teens.option_d: On the third day when the men were sore from circumcision
teens.explanation: On the third day when they were sore, Simeon and Levi came upon the city. See Genesis 34:25.

teens.question: Did Shechem genuinely love Dinah according to the text?
teens.option_a: Yes, his soul clave unto Dinah and he loved the damsel
teens.explanation: His soul clave unto Dinah and he loved the damsel. See Genesis 34:3.

teens.question: What was Jacob's main concern about Simeon and Levi's actions?
teens.option_c: That the Canaanites and Perizzites would gather against him and destroy his household
teens.explanation: Jacob feared the surrounding peoples would destroy him. See Genesis 34:30.

adults.question: What title is given to Hamor in this chapter?
adults.option_b: Chief priest
adults.option_d: The Hivite, prince of the country
adults.explanation: Hamor is called the Hivite, prince of the country. See Genesis 34:2.

adults.question: What economic incentive did Hamor offer Jacob's family?
adults.option_b: Free livestock
adults.option_c: Trade, dwell, and get possessions in the land
adults.explanation: Hamor proposed they trade, dwell, and get possessions. See Genesis 34:10.

adults.question: What did Shechem offer as bride price for Dinah?
adults.option_b: Ask me never so much dowry and gift, and I will give according as ye shall say
adults.explanation: Shechem said "Ask me never so much dowry and gift." See Genesis 34:12.

adults.question: The text says Jacob's sons answered Shechem and Hamor how?
adults.option_a: Deceitfully, because Shechem had defiled Dinah their sister
adults.option_b: Honestly
adults.explanation: The sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor deceitfully. See Genesis 34:13.

adults.question: What argument did Hamor and Shechem use to persuade their own city's men to be circumcised?
adults.option_b: Military alliance
adults.option_c: Shall not their cattle and substance and every beast of theirs be ours
adults.option_d: To honor the God of Jacob
adults.explanation: They told the men Jacob's wealth would become theirs. See Genesis 34:23.

adults.question: Besides killing the men, what else did Jacob's sons do to the city?
adults.option_a: They took the sheep, oxen, asses, wealth, children, and wives as spoil
adults.option_b: Nothing else
adults.explanation: They took all their wealth, little ones, and wives as spoil. See Genesis 34:28-29.

adults.question: How did Simeon and Levi justify their actions?
adults.option_b: They blamed Jacob for inaction
adults.option_d: They said should he deal with our sister as with an harlot
adults.explanation: They responded "Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot?" See Genesis 34:31.

adults.question: Jacob later referenced this event in his deathbed blessing. What did he say about Simeon and Levi?
adults.option_b: He cursed their anger and wrath, saying he would divide and scatter them in Israel
adults.explanation: Jacob cursed their fierce anger in Genesis 49:5-7, connecting to this event.

kids.question: Where did God tell Jacob to go and build an altar?
kids.explanation: God said unto Jacob "Arise, go up to Bethel, and make there an altar." See Genesis 35:1.

kids.question: What was the name of Rachel's second son, born in this chapter?
kids.explanation: Rachel bore a son and named him Benjamin. See Genesis 35:18.

kids.question: What happened to Rachel in this chapter?
kids.option_c: She died giving birth to Benjamin
kids.explanation: Rachel died in childbirth and was buried on the way to Ephrath. See Genesis 35:19.

teens.question: What did Jacob tell his household to do before going to Bethel?
teens.option_d: Put away the strange gods, be clean, and change their garments
teens.explanation: Jacob told them to put away strange gods and be clean. See Genesis 35:2.

teens.question: What did Jacob do with the strange gods and earrings his family gave him?
teens.option_b: He hid them under the oak tree at Shechem
teens.option_c: He threw them in a river
teens.explanation: Jacob hid them under the oak which was by Shechem. See Genesis 35:4.

teens.question: What name did Rachel give her son before she died?
teens.option_d: Benoni, meaning son of my sorrow
teens.explanation: Rachel called him Ben-oni but Jacob named him Benjamin. See Genesis 35:18.

teens.question: How old was Isaac when he died?
teens.explanation: Isaac was an hundred and fourscore (180) years old when he died. See Genesis 35:28.

teens.question: Who buried Isaac?
teens.explanation: His sons Esau and Jacob buried him. See Genesis 35:29.

adults.question: What supernatural protection did God provide Jacob on the journey to Bethel?
adults.option_b: Invisibility
adults.option_d: The terror of God was upon the cities round about so they did not pursue Jacob's sons
adults.explanation: The terror of God fell on surrounding cities so they did not pursue. See Genesis 35:5.

adults.question: What name did God give the place at Bethel where He spoke to Jacob?
adults.option_b: Peniel
adults.explanation: Jacob called the place El-bethel. See Genesis 35:7.

adults.question: Who died and was buried beneath the oak below Bethel?
adults.option_b: Deborah, Rebekah's nurse
adults.explanation: Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died and was buried below Bethel. See Genesis 35:8.

adults.question: God reaffirmed to Jacob at Bethel that his name was now Israel. What additional promise was given?
adults.option_a: A nation and a company of nations shall come from thee, and kings shall come out of thy loins
adults.option_b: Wealth beyond measure
adults.explanation: God promised nations and kings from Jacob's line. See Genesis 35:11.

adults.question: What does Benjamin mean, as named by Jacob?
adults.option_b: Son of strength
adults.explanation: Benjamin means son of the right hand. See Genesis 35:18.

adults.question: What sin did Reuben commit in this chapter?
adults.option_a: He lay with Bilhah his father's concubine, and Israel heard of it
adults.option_b: He stole from Jacob
adults.explanation: Reuben lay with Bilhah, and Israel heard it. See Genesis 35:22.

adults.question: How many sons of Jacob are listed in 35:23-26, and which mothers bore them?
adults.option_b: Twelve sons by two mothers
adults.option_d: Twelve sons by four mothers: Leah (6), Rachel (2), Bilhah (2), Zilpah (2)
adults.explanation: Twelve sons are listed from Leah, Rachel, Bilhah, and Zilpah. See Genesis 35:23-26.

adults.question: Where was Rachel buried?
adults.option_b: In the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem
adults.explanation: Rachel was buried in the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem. See Genesis 35:19.

kids.question: Whose family line is described in Genesis 36?
kids.explanation: Genesis 36 records the generations of Esau, who is Edom. See Genesis 36:1.

kids.question: What other name is Esau known by?
kids.explanation: Esau is also called Edom. See Genesis 36:1.

kids.question: Why did Esau move away from Jacob?
kids.option_c: Their riches were too great for them to dwell together
kids.explanation: Their substance was too great for them to dwell together. See Genesis 36:7.

teens.question: Where did Esau settle?
teens.explanation: Esau dwelt in mount Seir. See Genesis 36:8.

teens.question: What type of leaders came from Esau's line?
teens.explanation: The chapter lists dukes and kings that reigned in Edom. See Genesis 36:15-19, 31-39.

teens.question: What is significant about Edom having kings before Israel?
teens.option_d: Kings reigned in Edom before there reigned any king over the children of Israel
teens.explanation: These are the kings that reigned in Edom before any king reigned over Israel. See Genesis 36:31.

teens.question: Who was Eliphaz in relation to Esau?
teens.explanation: Eliphaz was the son of Esau's wife Adah. See Genesis 36:10.

teens.question: Who was Amalek in relation to Esau?
teens.option_c: His grandson through Eliphaz and his concubine Timna
teens.explanation: Amalek was the son of Eliphaz by Timna. See Genesis 36:12.

adults.question: How many dukes (chiefs) of Esau are listed in Genesis 36:40-43?
adults.option_b: Twenty
adults.explanation: Eleven dukes are listed in verses 40-43. See Genesis 36:40-43.

adults.question: Who were the Horites mentioned in this chapter?
adults.option_b: Egyptian settlers
adults.option_c: The original inhabitants of Seir whom Esau's descendants displaced
adults.explanation: The Horites were the earlier inhabitants of Seir. See Genesis 36:20-21.

adults.question: What is Anah remembered for in Genesis 36:24?
adults.option_b: Finding the mules (hot springs) in the wilderness as he fed his father's asses
adults.explanation: Anah found the mules in the wilderness while feeding Zibeon's asses. See Genesis 36:24.

adults.question: Which of Esau's wives was the daughter of Ishmael?
adults.option_b: Adah
adults.explanation: Bashemath was Ishmael's daughter. See Genesis 36:3.

adults.question: What nation would later descend from Amalek and become a persistent enemy of Israel?
adults.option_b: The Moabites
adults.explanation: Amalek fathered the Amalekites, perpetual enemies of Israel. See Genesis 36:12 and Exodus 17:8-16.

adults.question: How does Genesis 36 structurally function in the narrative?
adults.option_a: It closes out Esau's line before shifting focus entirely to Jacob and Joseph
adults.option_b: It introduces a new storyline
adults.option_c: It provides a genealogy of Israel
adults.option_d: It describes the conquest of Canaan
adults.explanation: The chapter concludes Esau's account before the Joseph narrative begins in chapter 37.

adults.question: Who was Oholibamah the daughter of?
adults.option_b: Beeri
adults.option_d: Anah the daughter of Zibeon the Hivite
adults.explanation: Aholibamah was the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon. See Genesis 36:2.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of recording Edom's prosperity alongside Israel's story?
adults.option_b: To show God blessed Esau materially as well, fulfilling Isaac's blessing, even though the covenant line went through Jacob
adults.explanation: Esau prospered as Isaac blessed, but the covenant continued through Jacob. See Genesis 27:39-40 and 36:1-43.

kids.question: What special gift did Jacob give to Joseph?
kids.explanation: Israel made Joseph a coat of many colours. See Genesis 37:3.

kids.question: What did Joseph dream about?
kids.option_b: Sheaves of grain bowing down to his sheaf, and the sun, moon, and stars bowing to him
kids.explanation: Joseph dreamed of sheaves bowing and sun, moon, and stars bowing. See Genesis 37:7-9.

kids.question: What did Joseph's brothers do to him?
kids.option_c: They sold him to merchants going to Egypt
kids.explanation: The brothers sold Joseph to Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. See Genesis 37:28.

teens.question: Why did Joseph's brothers hate him?
teens.option_d: Their father loved Joseph more than them and Joseph had dreams of ruling over them
teens.explanation: They hated him because of the coat and his dreams. See Genesis 37:4, 8.

teens.question: How old was Joseph when he was sold into slavery?
teens.explanation: Joseph was seventeen years old. See Genesis 37:2.

teens.question: Which brother tried to save Joseph from being killed?
teens.explanation: Reuben said to shed no blood but cast him into a pit, intending to rescue him later. See Genesis 37:21-22.

teens.question: How much was Joseph sold for?
teens.explanation: They sold Joseph for twenty pieces of silver. See Genesis 37:28.

teens.question: What did the brothers tell Jacob had happened to Joseph?
teens.option_c: They showed him the coat dipped in goat's blood and said a wild beast must have killed him
teens.explanation: They dipped the coat in goat blood and brought it to Jacob. See Genesis 37:31-33.

adults.question: What did Joseph bring to his father about his brothers?
adults.option_b: A request to leave
adults.explanation: Joseph brought unto his father their evil report. See Genesis 37:2.

adults.question: In Joseph's second dream, what represented his father, mother, and brothers?
adults.option_b: Twelve sheaves of grain
adults.option_c: The sun, moon, and eleven stars
adults.explanation: The sun, moon, and eleven stars bowed to Joseph. See Genesis 37:9.

adults.question: How did Jacob respond to Joseph's second dream?
adults.option_b: He rebuked Joseph but observed (kept) the saying
adults.option_d: He told him to stop dreaming
adults.explanation: His father rebuked him but observed the saying. See Genesis 37:10-11.

adults.question: Where were the brothers pasturing when Joseph was sent to find them?
adults.option_b: Bethel
adults.explanation: They went from Shechem to Dothan. See Genesis 37:12-17.

adults.question: Which brother suggested selling Joseph rather than killing him?
adults.option_b: Simeon
adults.explanation: Judah said "What profit is it if we slay our brother?" See Genesis 37:26.

adults.question: Who were the merchants that bought Joseph?
adults.option_b: Egyptians
adults.explanation: Ishmaelite/Midianite merchantmen bought Joseph. See Genesis 37:28.

adults.question: To whom was Joseph sold in Egypt?
adults.option_b: A wealthy merchant
adults.option_d: Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard
adults.explanation: The Midianites sold Joseph to Potiphar in Egypt. See Genesis 37:36.

adults.question: How does the selling of Joseph for twenty pieces of silver prefigure a later biblical event?
adults.option_b: Christ was betrayed for thirty pieces of silver, both involving jealousy and the selling of a chosen one
adults.explanation: Joseph sold for silver by jealous brothers parallels Christ's betrayal. See Genesis 37:28 and Matthew 26:15.

kids.question: Which of Jacob's sons is the main person in Genesis 38?
kids.explanation: Genesis 38 tells the story of Judah and his family. See Genesis 38:1.

kids.question: How many sons did Judah have at the start of this chapter?
kids.explanation: Judah had three sons: Er, Onan, and Shelah. See Genesis 38:3-5.

kids.question: What were the names of the twin boys born at the end of this chapter?
kids.explanation: The twins were Pharez (Perez) and Zarah (Zerah). See Genesis 38:29-30.

teens.question: Why did Er, Judah's firstborn, die?
teens.option_d: He was wicked in the sight of the LORD and the LORD slew him
teens.explanation: Er was wicked in the sight of the LORD, and the LORD slew him. See Genesis 38:7.

teens.question: Why was Onan supposed to marry Tamar?
teens.option_b: To raise up seed for his deceased brother Er as was the custom
teens.explanation: Judah told Onan to marry Tamar to raise up seed for Er. See Genesis 38:8.

teens.question: Why did Judah not give his son Shelah to Tamar?
teens.option_d: He was afraid Shelah would also die like his brothers
teens.explanation: Judah feared lest Shelah die also, as his brethren did. See Genesis 38:11.

teens.question: How did Tamar get Judah to notice her?
teens.option_a: She disguised herself with a veil and sat by the road
teens.option_d: She asked a friend to introduce them
teens.explanation: Tamar covered herself with a veil and sat in an open place. See Genesis 38:14.

teens.question: What happened during the birth of the twins?
teens.option_c: Zarah put out his hand first with a scarlet thread, but Pharez broke forth first
teens.explanation: Zarah's hand came first but Pharez actually broke forth first. See Genesis 38:28-30.

adults.question: What three items did Tamar request as a pledge from Judah?
adults.option_b: His ring, sword, and shield
adults.explanation: Tamar asked for his signet, bracelets, and staff. See Genesis 38:18.

adults.question: What was Judah's initial reaction when told Tamar was pregnant?
adults.option_b: He ignored the news
adults.option_c: He said bring her forth and let her be burnt
adults.option_d: He asked who the father was
adults.explanation: Judah said "Bring her forth, and let her be burnt." See Genesis 38:24.

adults.question: How did Tamar prove Judah was the father?
adults.option_b: She sent the signet, bracelets, and staff saying the owner of these is the father
adults.explanation: Tamar sent the pledged items to Judah for identification. See Genesis 38:25.

adults.question: What did Judah confess when confronted with the evidence?
adults.option_a: She hath been more righteous than I because I gave her not to Shelah my son
adults.option_b: He denied everything
adults.explanation: Judah admitted Tamar was more righteous than he. See Genesis 38:26.

adults.question: Why is Pharez significant in biblical genealogy?
adults.option_b: He was the firstborn of all Jacob's grandchildren
adults.option_c: He is in the lineage of David and ultimately Jesus Christ
adults.explanation: Pharez appears in the genealogy of David and Christ. See Ruth 4:18-22 and Matthew 1:3.

adults.question: Who was Judah's wife and what was her background?
adults.option_a: The daughter of Shuah, a Canaanite
adults.option_b: Tamar, an Israelite
adults.explanation: Judah married the daughter of a certain Canaanite named Shuah. See Genesis 38:2.

adults.question: Who did Judah send to retrieve his pledge from the woman by the road?
adults.option_b: His son Shelah
adults.explanation: Judah sent his friend Hirah the Adullamite. See Genesis 38:20.

adults.question: Why does this chapter interrupt the Joseph narrative between chapters 37 and 39?
adults.option_b: It contrasts Judah's moral failure in Canaan with Joseph's integrity in Egypt and establishes the Messianic line through Pharez
adults.option_c: It has no connection to the surrounding text
adults.option_d: It was added later by editors
adults.explanation: The chapter contrasts Judah with Joseph and establishes the line to David and Christ.

kids.question: Who bought Joseph as a slave in Egypt?
kids.explanation: Joseph was bought by Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh. See Genesis 39:1.

kids.question: Was God with Joseph in Egypt?
kids.option_b: Yes, the LORD was with Joseph and made everything he did successful
kids.explanation: The LORD was with Joseph and he was a prosperous man. See Genesis 39:2.

kids.question: What happened to Joseph at the end of this chapter?
kids.explanation: Joseph's master put him into the prison. See Genesis 39:20.

teens.question: What position did Potiphar give Joseph in his household?
teens.option_d: Overseer of all that he had
teens.explanation: Potiphar made Joseph overseer over his house and all that he had. See Genesis 39:4-5.

teens.question: What did Potiphar's wife repeatedly ask Joseph to do?
teens.explanation: Potiphar's wife said to Joseph "Lie with me." See Genesis 39:7.

teens.question: What reason did Joseph give for refusing?
teens.option_b: He was too busy with work
teens.option_c: He did not care for her
teens.option_d: How can I do this great wickedness and sin against God
teens.explanation: Joseph said "how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?" See Genesis 39:9.

teens.question: What did Potiphar's wife use as false evidence against Joseph?
teens.option_a: His garment that he left behind when he fled
teens.explanation: She kept his garment and showed it as evidence. See Genesis 39:12-18.

teens.question: Even in prison, how did God show favour to Joseph?
teens.option_a: He sent an angel to free him
