teens.option_c: The LORD gave him favour with the keeper of the prison who committed everything to his hand
teens.explanation: The LORD gave Joseph favour with the prison keeper. See Genesis 39:21-23.

adults.question: How is Joseph physically described in this chapter?
adults.option_b: No description is given
adults.option_d: A goodly person and well favoured
adults.explanation: Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured. See Genesis 39:6.

adults.question: What comprehensive reason did Joseph give for refusing Potiphar's wife?
adults.option_b: Cultural propriety
adults.option_c: His master trusted him with everything except his wife and it would be wickedness and sin against God
adults.explanation: Joseph cited his master's trust and that it would be sin against God. See Genesis 39:8-9.

adults.question: How did God bless Potiphar's house because of Joseph?
adults.option_b: The blessing of the LORD was upon all that he had in the house and in the field
adults.explanation: The LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake. See Genesis 39:5.

adults.question: What role did Joseph assume in the prison?
adults.option_a: The keeper committed all the prisoners to Joseph's hand and whatever was done there he was the doer of it
adults.option_b: He planned escapes
adults.explanation: The prison keeper committed everything to Joseph's hand. See Genesis 39:22.

adults.question: What pattern is established by Joseph prospering in both Potiphar's house and prison?
adults.option_b: Joseph was a skilled negotiator
adults.option_c: Wherever Joseph went the LORD was with him and made all that he did to prosper
adults.explanation: The LORD was with Joseph and prospered him in both settings. See Genesis 39:2-3, 21-23.

adults.question: How persistent was Potiphar's wife in her temptation?
adults.option_a: She spake to Joseph day by day but he hearkened not unto her
adults.option_b: She asked only once
adults.option_c: She tried twice then gave up
adults.option_d: She sent others to persuade him
adults.explanation: She spoke to Joseph day by day. See Genesis 39:10.

adults.question: What accusation did Potiphar's wife make to the men of the house?
adults.option_b: That Joseph worshipped foreign gods
adults.option_d: That her husband brought in a Hebrew to mock us
adults.explanation: She said her husband brought in a Hebrew to mock them. See Genesis 39:14.

adults.question: How does Joseph's unjust imprisonment serve the larger divine plan?
adults.option_b: It placed Joseph in the same prison as Pharaoh's butler and baker, setting up his rise to power
adults.explanation: Prison positioned Joseph to meet Pharaoh's servants and interpret dreams. See Genesis 39:20 and chapters 40-41.

kids.question: Who else was in prison with Joseph?
kids.explanation: Pharaoh's butler and baker were put in the prison with Joseph. See Genesis 40:1-3.

kids.question: What special ability did God give Joseph in prison?
kids.option_b: The ability to understand the meaning of dreams
kids.explanation: Joseph said "Do not interpretations belong to God?" and interpreted their dreams. See Genesis 40:8.

kids.question: What happened to the butler after Joseph interpreted his dream?
kids.option_c: He was restored to his position serving Pharaoh
kids.explanation: The chief butler was restored unto his butlership. See Genesis 40:21.

teens.question: What did the butler dream about?
teens.option_d: A vine with three branches that budded, blossomed, and produced ripe grapes which he pressed into Pharaoh's cup
teens.explanation: The butler dreamed of a vine with three branches and grapes pressed into Pharaoh's cup. See Genesis 40:9-11.

teens.question: What did the baker dream about?
teens.option_b: Three white baskets on his head with baked goods that birds ate from the top basket
teens.explanation: The baker dreamed of three baskets with birds eating from the top one. See Genesis 40:16-17.

teens.question: What did the three branches and three baskets represent?
teens.explanation: Both sets of three represented three days. See Genesis 40:12-13, 18-19.

teens.question: What happened to the baker?
teens.option_a: Pharaoh hanged him, as Joseph had interpreted
teens.option_b: He was restored to his job
teens.option_d: He was sent to another land
teens.explanation: The chief baker was hanged as Joseph had interpreted. See Genesis 40:22.

teens.question: What favour did Joseph ask of the butler?
teens.option_c: To remember him and mention him to Pharaoh so he could get out of prison
teens.option_d: To send a message to his family
teens.explanation: Joseph asked the butler to think on him and make mention to Pharaoh. See Genesis 40:14.

adults.question: What did Joseph say about the source of dream interpretation?
adults.option_a: It came from his own wisdom
adults.option_b: He read it in scrolls
adults.option_c: He learned it from the Egyptians
adults.option_d: Do not interpretations belong to God? Tell me your dreams
adults.explanation: Joseph said "Do not interpretations belong to God?" See Genesis 40:8.

adults.question: What detail did Joseph share about his own background when speaking to the butler?
adults.option_b: That he was a prince
adults.option_c: That he was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews and had done nothing to deserve the dungeon
adults.explanation: Joseph said he was stolen from the land of the Hebrews. See Genesis 40:15.

adults.question: What occasion prompted the butler and baker's release from prison?
adults.option_b: Pharaoh's birthday
adults.explanation: The third day was Pharaoh's birthday and he made a feast. See Genesis 40:20.

adults.question: What did the chief butler fail to do after being restored?
adults.option_a: He did not remember Joseph but forgat him
adults.option_b: Pay Joseph
adults.option_d: Return to prison to help others
adults.explanation: The chief butler did not remember Joseph, but forgat him. See Genesis 40:23.

adults.question: Why did the baker decide to share his dream with Joseph?
adults.option_b: Joseph asked him
adults.option_c: He saw that the interpretation of the butler's dream was good
adults.explanation: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good, he shared his dream. See Genesis 40:16.

adults.question: What specific detail of the baker's dream foretold his death?
adults.option_a: The birds did eat them out of the basket upon his head, meaning Pharaoh would hang him and birds would eat his flesh
adults.option_b: Empty baskets
adults.explanation: Birds eating from the top basket symbolized birds eating his flesh. See Genesis 40:17-19.

adults.question: How long did Joseph remain in prison after the butler was restored before the events of chapter 41?
adults.option_b: Three years
adults.explanation: At the end of two full years Pharaoh dreamed. See Genesis 41:1.

adults.question: How does the butler's forgetfulness serve the narrative theologically?
adults.option_b: It demonstrates that Joseph's deliverance would come from God's timing through Pharaoh's dreams, not through human favour
adults.explanation: God's timing ensured Joseph's release would coincide with Egypt's crisis. See Genesis 40:23 and 41:1.

kids.question: What did Pharaoh dream about?
kids.option_a: Seven fat cows eaten by seven thin cows, and seven good ears of corn eaten by seven thin ears
kids.explanation: Pharaoh dreamed of seven fat cows, seven thin cows, seven good ears, and seven thin ears. See Genesis 41:1-7.

kids.question: Who told Pharaoh that Joseph could interpret dreams?
kids.explanation: The chief butler finally remembered Joseph and told Pharaoh. See Genesis 41:9-13.

kids.question: What important job did Pharaoh give to Joseph?
kids.option_c: Ruler over all the land of Egypt
kids.explanation: Pharaoh set Joseph over all the land of Egypt. See Genesis 41:41.

teens.question: What did the seven fat cows and seven good ears of corn represent?
teens.option_d: Seven years of great plenty in Egypt
teens.explanation: The seven good cows and ears were seven years of plenty. See Genesis 41:29.

teens.question: What did the seven thin cows and seven thin ears represent?
teens.option_b: Seven years of famine that would follow the plenty
teens.explanation: The seven lean cows and thin ears were seven years of famine. See Genesis 41:30.

teens.question: What plan did Joseph recommend to Pharaoh?
teens.option_d: Appoint a wise man to store one fifth of the harvest during the seven good years
teens.explanation: Joseph advised storing a fifth of the produce during the plentiful years. See Genesis 41:33-36.

teens.question: What Egyptian name did Pharaoh give Joseph?
teens.explanation: Pharaoh called Joseph Zaphnath-paaneah. See Genesis 41:45.

teens.question: How old was Joseph when he stood before Pharaoh?
teens.explanation: Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh. See Genesis 41:46.

adults.question: What did Joseph say to Pharaoh about who interprets dreams?
adults.option_b: He attributed it to Egyptian learning
adults.option_c: He said it was a natural ability
adults.option_d: It is not in me; God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace
adults.explanation: Joseph said "It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace." See Genesis 41:16.

adults.question: Why did Pharaoh say the dream was doubled (shown twice)?
adults.option_b: It was a coincidence
adults.option_c: Because the thing is established by God and God will shortly bring it to pass
adults.explanation: Joseph explained the dream was doubled because it was established by God. See Genesis 41:32.

adults.question: What symbols of authority did Pharaoh give Joseph?
adults.option_b: His ring, vestures of fine linen, and a gold chain about his neck
adults.explanation: Pharaoh gave Joseph his ring, fine linen, and a gold chain. See Genesis 41:42.

adults.question: Who was Joseph's wife and who was her father?
adults.option_a: Asenath, daughter of Potipherah priest of On
adults.option_b: An Israelite woman
adults.explanation: Pharaoh gave Joseph Asenath the daughter of Poti-pherah priest of On. See Genesis 41:45.

adults.question: What were the names of Joseph's two sons and what did their names mean?
adults.option_b: Perez and Zerah
adults.option_c: Manasseh (God made me forget my toil) and Ephraim (God hath caused me to be fruitful)
adults.explanation: Manasseh means forgetting and Ephraim means fruitful. See Genesis 41:51-52.

adults.question: How extensive was the famine according to the text?
adults.option_a: The famine was over all the face of the earth and all countries came to Egypt to buy corn
adults.option_b: Only in Egypt
adults.explanation: The famine was in all lands and all countries came to Joseph. See Genesis 41:56-57.

adults.question: What did the people cry to Pharaoh when the famine began?
adults.option_b: They rioted
adults.option_d: Pharaoh told them to go unto Joseph and do whatever he said
adults.explanation: Pharaoh said "Go unto Joseph; what he saith to you, do." See Genesis 41:55.

adults.question: What was proclaimed before Joseph's chariot as he rode through Egypt?
adults.option_b: Bow the knee (Abrech)
adults.explanation: They cried before him "Bow the knee" (Abrech). See Genesis 41:43.

kids.question: Why did Joseph's brothers go to Egypt?
kids.option_a: To buy grain because there was a famine
kids.explanation: Jacob sent his sons to Egypt to buy corn because of the famine. See Genesis 42:1-3.

kids.question: Did the brothers recognize Joseph when they saw him?
kids.option_b: No, they did not recognize him
kids.explanation: Joseph knew his brethren but they knew not him. See Genesis 42:8.

kids.question: Which brother did Jacob refuse to send to Egypt?
kids.explanation: Jacob would not send Benjamin lest mischief befall him. See Genesis 42:4.

teens.question: What did Joseph accuse his brothers of being?
teens.option_d: Spies who came to see the nakedness of the land
teens.explanation: Joseph said "Ye are spies; to see the nakedness of the land ye are come." See Genesis 42:9.

teens.question: Which brother was kept behind in Egypt as a hostage?
teens.explanation: Joseph took Simeon and bound him before their eyes. See Genesis 42:24.

teens.question: What did the brothers find in their sacks on the way home?
teens.option_d: Their money had been returned in the mouths of their sacks
teens.explanation: Every man's money was in the mouth of his sack. See Genesis 42:27-28.

teens.question: How did Jacob react when the brothers asked to take Benjamin?
teens.option_a: He refused and said they had bereaved him of his children
teens.option_d: He suggested sending a servant instead
teens.explanation: Jacob said "Me have ye bereaved of my children." See Genesis 42:36.

teens.question: What did Joseph do privately when he heard his brothers speaking about their guilt?
teens.option_c: He turned himself about from them and wept
teens.explanation: Joseph turned from them and wept. See Genesis 42:24.

adults.question: How many brothers came to Egypt the first time?
adults.option_b: Nine
adults.explanation: Ten of Joseph's brethren went down to buy corn. See Genesis 42:3.

adults.question: What earlier event did the brothers recall that caused them guilt?
adults.option_b: Lying to Isaac
adults.option_c: They said they were guilty concerning their brother Joseph because they saw the anguish of his soul when he besought them and they would not hear
adults.explanation: They recalled seeing Joseph's anguish and ignoring his pleas. See Genesis 42:21.

adults.question: What did Reuben say to his brothers about their guilt?
adults.option_b: Spake I not unto you saying do not sin against the child and ye would not hear; therefore his blood is required
adults.explanation: Reuben reminded them he had warned them not to sin against the child. See Genesis 42:22.

adults.question: Why did Joseph speak through an interpreter with his brothers?
adults.option_a: To maintain his disguise since they knew not that Joseph understood them
adults.option_b: He had forgotten Hebrew
adults.explanation: Joseph used an interpreter and they knew not he understood them. See Genesis 42:23.

adults.question: What condition did Joseph set for proving the brothers were not spies?
adults.option_b: They must serve in Egypt
adults.option_c: They must bring their youngest brother to him
adults.explanation: Joseph demanded they bring their youngest brother. See Genesis 42:15-20.

adults.question: What desperate offer did Reuben make to Jacob to persuade him to release Benjamin?
adults.option_a: Slay my two sons if I bring him not to thee
adults.option_b: All his possessions
adults.option_c: He would stay in Egypt instead
adults.explanation: Reuben said "Slay my two sons, if I bring him not to thee." See Genesis 42:37.

adults.question: How did Jacob describe his state when refusing to send Benjamin?
adults.option_b: He was hopeful
adults.option_d: He said if mischief befall Benjamin ye shall bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave
adults.explanation: Jacob feared his gray hairs would go down to the grave in sorrow. See Genesis 42:38.

adults.question: What does Joseph's testing of his brothers reveal about his character?
adults.option_b: He was testing whether they had changed and would now protect the youngest brother Benjamin as they had failed to protect him
adults.explanation: Joseph tested whether his brothers would sacrifice Benjamin as they had sacrificed him. See Genesis 42:15-20.

kids.question: Why did the brothers have to go back to Egypt?
kids.option_a: They ran out of food and needed more grain
kids.explanation: The famine was sore and they had eaten all the corn they brought. See Genesis 43:1-2.

kids.question: Who went with the brothers on the second trip that did not go the first time?
kids.explanation: Jacob finally allowed Benjamin to go. See Genesis 43:13.

kids.question: When Joseph invited his brothers to eat at his house, how much more food did Benjamin get than the others?
kids.explanation: Benjamin's mess was five times so much as any of theirs. See Genesis 43:34.

teens.question: Which brother pledged to be personally responsible for Benjamin's safety?
teens.explanation: Judah said "I will be surety for him; of my hand shalt thou require him." See Genesis 43:9.

teens.question: What gifts did Jacob send with the brothers to the ruler of Egypt?
teens.option_b: Balm, honey, spices, myrrh, nuts, and almonds plus double money
teens.explanation: Jacob sent a present of balm, honey, spices, myrrh, nuts, and almonds. See Genesis 43:11.

teens.question: What did the brothers fear when they were brought to Joseph's house?
teens.option_c: They would never see Canaan again
teens.option_d: They would be made bondmen because of the money found in their sacks
teens.explanation: They feared being made slaves because of the money in their sacks. See Genesis 43:18.

teens.question: What did Joseph's steward say about the money in the sacks?
teens.option_a: Peace be to you; your God and the God of your father hath given you treasure; I had your money
teens.explanation: The steward said their God gave them treasure and that he had their money. See Genesis 43:23.

teens.question: What happened when Joseph saw Benjamin?
teens.option_c: His bowels did yearn upon his brother and he sought a place to weep
teens.explanation: Joseph's bowels yearned and he sought a place to weep. See Genesis 43:30.

adults.question: What argument did Judah use to convince Jacob to send Benjamin?
adults.option_b: He appealed to God
adults.option_c: He threatened to go without permission
adults.option_d: If they had not lingered they could have gone and returned twice by now; Judah personally guaranteed Benjamin's safety
adults.explanation: Judah guaranteed Benjamin and pointed out the delay. See Genesis 43:8-10.

adults.question: What resigned statement did Jacob make when finally agreeing to send Benjamin?
adults.option_b: I trust you completely
adults.option_c: If I be bereaved of my children, I am bereaved
adults.explanation: Jacob said "If I be bereaved of my children, I am bereaved." See Genesis 43:14.

adults.question: How did Joseph's brothers present themselves when they arrived at his house?
adults.option_b: They bowed down to him to the earth with their present
adults.explanation: They bowed themselves to him to the earth. See Genesis 43:26.

adults.question: What question did Joseph ask about their father?
adults.option_a: Is your father well? Is he yet alive?
adults.option_b: Where does he live
adults.explanation: Joseph asked "Is your father well, the old man of whom ye spake? Is he yet alive?" See Genesis 43:27.

adults.question: What blessing did Joseph pronounce over Benjamin?
adults.option_b: May you inherit the land
adults.option_c: God be gracious unto thee, my son
adults.explanation: Joseph said "God be gracious unto thee, my son." See Genesis 43:29.

adults.question: Why did the Egyptians eat separately from the Hebrews at the feast?
adults.option_a: Because the Egyptians might not eat bread with the Hebrews, for that is an abomination unto them
adults.option_b: Joseph ordered it
adults.option_d: It was a custom of politeness
adults.explanation: It was an abomination for Egyptians to eat with Hebrews. See Genesis 43:32.

adults.question: What astonished the brothers about the seating arrangement at the feast?
adults.option_b: They sat on golden chairs
adults.option_d: They were seated in order of age from firstborn to youngest and they marvelled
adults.explanation: They were set in birth order and the men marvelled one at another. See Genesis 43:33.

adults.question: What is the significance of Benjamin receiving five times the portion of the others?
adults.option_b: It tested whether the brothers would be jealous of favouritism toward Rachel's son, as they had been jealous of Joseph
adults.explanation: The fivefold portion tested whether the brothers had overcome jealousy of Rachel's sons. See Genesis 43:34.

kids.question: What was secretly placed in Benjamin's sack?
kids.explanation: Joseph's steward put the silver cup in Benjamin's sack. See Genesis 44:2.

kids.question: Were the brothers telling the truth when they said they did not steal?
kids.option_b: Yes, they did not know the cup was placed there
kids.option_c: Only some of them were honest
kids.explanation: The brothers were innocent; the cup was planted by Joseph's steward. See Genesis 44:1-2.

kids.question: Which brother begged to stay as a slave instead of Benjamin?
kids.explanation: Judah offered to be a servant instead of Benjamin. See Genesis 44:33.

teens.question: What did the brothers do when the cup was found in Benjamin's sack?
teens.option_d: They rent their clothes and returned to the city
teens.explanation: They rent their clothes, laded their asses, and returned to the city. See Genesis 44:13.

teens.question: What bold claim had the brothers made before the search?
teens.option_b: Let him with whom the cup is found die, and the rest would be bondmen
teens.explanation: They said the thief should die and the rest be servants. See Genesis 44:9.

teens.question: What did Judah say would happen to Jacob if Benjamin did not return?
teens.option_d: Jacob would die, his gray hairs brought down with sorrow to the grave
teens.explanation: Judah said their father would die of grief. See Genesis 44:29-31.

teens.question: What did Judah offer as an alternative to keeping Benjamin?
teens.option_a: Let me abide instead of the lad as a bondman to my lord
teens.explanation: Judah offered himself as slave in place of Benjamin. See Genesis 44:33.

teens.question: Why was Judah's plea so important for what happened next?
teens.option_c: It showed the brothers had changed and would now sacrifice themselves for Benjamin, which moved Joseph deeply
teens.explanation: Judah's selfless plea proved the brothers had transformed. See Genesis 44:33-34.

adults.question: What did Joseph instruct his steward to put in each brother's sack besides grain?
adults.option_b: Egyptian trinkets
adults.option_d: Each man's money in the sack's mouth, and the silver cup in Benjamin's sack
adults.explanation: The steward put every man's money in his sack and the cup in Benjamin's. See Genesis 44:1-2.

adults.question: What did Joseph say about the stolen cup?
adults.option_b: It was worthless
adults.option_c: Is not this it in which my lord drinketh and whereby indeed he divineth
adults.explanation: The steward said it was used for drinking and divining. See Genesis 44:5.

adults.question: What did Joseph say when the brothers were brought back?
adults.option_b: What deed is this that ye have done? Wot ye not that such a man as I can certainly divine?
adults.explanation: Joseph asked what they had done, claiming he could divine. See Genesis 44:15.

adults.question: What did Judah acknowledge about their situation?
adults.option_a: God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants, acknowledging a deeper guilt beyond the cup
adults.option_b: They were completely innocent
adults.explanation: Judah said "God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants." See Genesis 44:16.

adults.question: How did Judah describe Jacob's relationship to Benjamin?
adults.option_b: Jacob did not care for Benjamin
adults.option_c: His father loveth him and the lad's brother is dead and he alone is left of his mother
adults.explanation: Judah explained Jacob's special love for the surviving son of Rachel. See Genesis 44:20.

adults.question: What phrase did Judah use to describe the bond between Jacob and Benjamin?
adults.option_a: His life is bound up in the lad's life
adults.option_b: Mere affection
adults.explanation: Judah said "his life is bound up in the lad's life." See Genesis 44:30.

adults.question: How does Judah's offer to substitute himself for Benjamin prefigure a biblical theme?
adults.option_b: It shows legal customs
adults.option_d: It prefigures substitutionary atonement, where one takes the place of another, pointing toward Christ
adults.explanation: Judah's willingness to take Benjamin's punishment prefigures substitutionary sacrifice. See Genesis 44:33.

adults.question: How had Judah changed from the man who suggested selling Joseph in chapter 37?
adults.option_a: He had not changed at all
adults.option_b: He went from callously selling one of Rachel's sons to offering his own freedom to save the other, showing profound moral transformation
adults.explanation: Judah's transformation from selling Joseph to sacrificing for Benjamin is a key character arc. See Genesis 37:26-27 and 44:33.

kids.question: What did Joseph finally tell his brothers?
kids.option_a: I am Joseph! Is my father still alive?
kids.option_b: He told them to leave Egypt
kids.option_c: He told them to bring gold
kids.explanation: Joseph said "I am Joseph; doth my father yet live?" See Genesis 45:3.

kids.question: Was Joseph angry at his brothers for selling him?
kids.option_b: No, he said God sent him before them to preserve life
kids.explanation: Joseph said "God did send me before you to preserve life." See Genesis 45:5.

kids.question: What did Joseph ask his brothers to do?
kids.option_c: Go get their father Jacob and bring him to Egypt
kids.explanation: Joseph said "Haste ye, and go up to my father, and bring him down." See Genesis 45:9, 13.

teens.question: How did the brothers react when Joseph revealed himself?
teens.option_d: They could not answer him because they were troubled at his presence
teens.explanation: His brethren could not answer him, for they were troubled at his presence. See Genesis 45:3.

teens.question: How did Joseph explain why he was in Egypt?
teens.option_b: God sent him before them to preserve a posterity and to save their lives by a great deliverance
teens.option_c: He said it was bad luck
teens.explanation: Joseph said God sent him to preserve a posterity and deliver them. See Genesis 45:7.

teens.question: Where did Joseph say his family should live in Egypt?
teens.explanation: Joseph told them to dwell in the land of Goshen. See Genesis 45:10.

teens.question: How many more years of famine remained according to Joseph?
teens.explanation: Joseph said there were yet five years of famine remaining. See Genesis 45:6, 11.

teens.question: How did Pharaoh react to the news that Joseph's brothers had come?
teens.option_c: It pleased Pharaoh and his servants well, and he offered wagons and provisions
teens.explanation: It pleased Pharaoh well and he commanded Joseph to give them wagons. See Genesis 45:16-19.

adults.question: What did Joseph do before revealing himself to ensure privacy?
adults.option_b: He sent a written note
adults.option_d: He caused every Egyptian to go out from him so no man stood with him
adults.explanation: Joseph cried "Cause every man to go out from me." See Genesis 45:1.

adults.question: What theological statement did Joseph make about God's sovereignty over his suffering?
adults.option_b: God was punishing the brothers
adults.option_c: It was not you that sent me hither but God who made him a father to Pharaoh and lord of all Egypt
adults.explanation: Joseph said "it was not you that sent me hither, but God." See Genesis 45:8.

adults.question: Joseph wept so loudly upon revealing himself that who heard it?
adults.option_b: The Egyptians and the house of Pharaoh heard
adults.explanation: Joseph wept aloud and the Egyptians and Pharaoh's house heard. See Genesis 45:2.

adults.question: What specific provision did Pharaoh offer Joseph's family?
adults.option_a: The good of all the land of Egypt and they would eat the fat of the land
adults.option_b: Gold and silver
adults.explanation: Pharaoh said "the good of all the land of Egypt is yours." See Genesis 45:18-20.

adults.question: What extra gifts did Joseph give Benjamin compared to the other brothers?
adults.option_b: Ten changes of raiment
adults.option_c: Three hundred pieces of silver and five changes of raiment versus one change each for the others
adults.explanation: Benjamin got 300 pieces of silver and 5 changes of raiment; others got 1 change each. See Genesis 45:22.

adults.question: What parting instruction did Joseph give his brothers?
adults.option_a: See that ye fall not out by the way, meaning do not quarrel on the journey
adults.option_b: Travel quickly
adults.explanation: Joseph said "See that ye fall not out by the way." See Genesis 45:24.

adults.question: How did Jacob respond when first told Joseph was alive?
adults.option_b: He praised God
adults.option_d: Jacob's heart fainted for he believed them not
adults.explanation: Jacob's heart fainted, for he believed them not. See Genesis 45:26.

adults.question: What finally convinced Jacob that Joseph was alive?
adults.option_b: When he saw the wagons Joseph had sent, the spirit of Jacob revived
adults.explanation: When Jacob saw the wagons, his spirit revived. See Genesis 45:27.

kids.question: Where did God speak to Jacob on his way to Egypt?
kids.explanation: God spake unto Israel in the visions of the night at Beer-sheba. See Genesis 46:1-2.

kids.question: How many of Jacob's family went to Egypt?
kids.explanation: All the souls of the house of Jacob which came into Egypt were threescore and ten (70). See Genesis 46:27.

kids.question: How did Joseph greet his father Jacob?
kids.option_b: He sent a servant to meet him
kids.option_c: He fell on his neck and wept a good while
kids.explanation: Joseph fell on Jacob's neck and wept on his neck a good while. See Genesis 46:29.

teens.question: What did God promise Jacob at Beersheba?
teens.option_a: He would return to Canaan immediately
teens.option_b: He would give him a long life
teens.option_d: He would make Jacob a great nation in Egypt and would surely bring him up again
teens.explanation: God promised to make Jacob a great nation there and to bring him up again. See Genesis 46:3-4.

teens.question: What comforting promise did God add about Joseph?
teens.option_b: Joseph shall put his hand upon thine eyes, meaning Joseph would be with Jacob when he died
teens.explanation: God said Joseph would put his hand upon Jacob's eyes. See Genesis 46:4.

teens.question: Which son did Jacob send ahead to prepare for the family's arrival?
teens.option_d: Judah, to direct the way to Goshen
teens.explanation: Jacob sent Judah before him unto Joseph to direct his face unto Goshen. See Genesis 46:28.

teens.question: What did Jacob say to Joseph when they were reunited?
teens.option_a: Now let me die, since I have seen thy face, because thou art yet alive
teens.option_c: Let us return to Canaan together
teens.option_d: I never thought this would happen
teens.explanation: Israel said "Now let me die, since I have seen thy face." See Genesis 46:30.

teens.question: What did Joseph tell his family to say to Pharaoh about their occupation?
teens.option_c: They were shepherds, because every shepherd is an abomination unto the Egyptians, which would help them settle separately in Goshen
teens.explanation: Joseph coached them to say they were shepherds so they could dwell in Goshen. See Genesis 46:33-34.

adults.question: What sacrifices did Jacob offer at Beersheba before continuing to Egypt?
adults.option_b: Peace offerings
adults.option_d: Sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac
adults.explanation: Israel offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac. See Genesis 46:1.

adults.question: How did God address Jacob in the vision?
adults.option_b: Son of Isaac
adults.explanation: God said "Jacob, Jacob" and he said "Here am I." See Genesis 46:2.

adults.question: What reassurance did God give about going to Egypt?
adults.option_b: Fear not to go down into Egypt for I will there make of thee a great nation
adults.explanation: God told Jacob not to fear because He would make a great nation there. See Genesis 46:3.

adults.question: The number seventy souls includes which notable grandchildren?
adults.option_a: Er and Onan (who died in Canaan), plus Joseph and his two sons already in Egypt
adults.option_b: Only sons
adults.explanation: The seventy includes Er and Onan who died, and Joseph, Manasseh, and Ephraim in Egypt. See Genesis 46:12, 20, 27.

adults.question: How many of the seventy souls came through Leah's line?
adults.option_b: Twenty
adults.explanation: The souls through Leah were thirty and three. See Genesis 46:15.

adults.question: Why was it strategically important for Jacob's family to settle in Goshen?
adults.option_a: Goshen was separate from mainstream Egyptian society, allowing them to maintain their identity as shepherds while being near Joseph
adults.option_b: It was the most fertile land
adults.explanation: Goshen provided separation to preserve their distinct identity. See Genesis 46:34.

adults.question: What is the significance of God saying "I will go down with thee into Egypt"?
adults.option_b: God would protect the journey
adults.option_d: God personally accompanying Jacob assured him that leaving the Promised Land was within God's plan, not abandoning the covenant
adults.explanation: God's presence affirmed that Egypt was part of His covenant plan. See Genesis 46:4.

adults.question: How does Jacob's descent into Egypt with seventy souls connect to Deuteronomy 10:22?
adults.option_b: Moses later referenced that their fathers went down to Egypt with seventy persons and God made them as the stars of heaven for multitude
adults.option_d: Only the number is the same
adults.explanation: Moses recalled the seventy who became a great multitude. See Genesis 46:27 and Deuteronomy 10:22.

kids.question: Where did Jacob's family settle in Egypt?
kids.explanation: Joseph placed his family in the land of Goshen (Rameses). See Genesis 47:11.

kids.question: Did Jacob meet Pharaoh?
kids.explanation: Joseph brought Jacob before Pharaoh and Jacob blessed Pharaoh. See Genesis 47:7.

kids.question: How old did Jacob say he was when Pharaoh asked?
kids.explanation: Jacob said "The days of the years of my pilgrimage are an hundred and thirty years." See Genesis 47:9.

teens.question: How did Jacob describe his life to Pharaoh?
teens.option_d: Few and evil have the days of the years of my life been
teens.explanation: Jacob said his days were few and evil. See Genesis 47:9.

teens.question: What did the Egyptians eventually give to Joseph in exchange for food during the famine?
teens.option_b: First their money, then livestock, then their land, and finally themselves as servants
teens.explanation: They gave money, then cattle, then land, then themselves. See Genesis 47:14-21.

teens.question: What percentage of crops did Joseph require Egyptians to give Pharaoh?
teens.explanation: Joseph made it a law to give the fifth part unto Pharaoh. See Genesis 47:26.

teens.question: Whose land was the only land not bought by Pharaoh?
teens.option_a: The priests' land, because they had a portion from Pharaoh
teens.explanation: Only the land of the priests bought he not, for they had a portion from Pharaoh. See Genesis 47:22.

teens.question: What request did Jacob make to Joseph near the end of his life?
teens.option_a: Build me a tomb in Egypt
teens.option_b: Give all my possessions to Benjamin
teens.option_c: Bury me not in Egypt but carry me out to be buried with my fathers
teens.option_d: Let me see Pharaoh one more time
teens.explanation: Jacob asked Joseph to bury him with his fathers, not in Egypt. See Genesis 47:29-30.

adults.question: How many of Joseph's brothers did he present to Pharaoh?
adults.option_b: Two brothers
adults.explanation: Joseph took five of his brethren and presented them to Pharaoh. See Genesis 47:2.

adults.question: How many times did Jacob bless Pharaoh?
adults.option_b: Three times
adults.explanation: Jacob blessed Pharaoh both when he came in and when he left. See Genesis 47:7, 10.

adults.question: Jacob said his years had not attained to the years of his fathers. How old were Abraham and Isaac when they died?
adults.option_b: Abraham 175, Isaac 180
adults.explanation: Abraham died at 175 (Genesis 25:7) and Isaac at 180 (Genesis 35:28). Jacob was 130 at this point.

adults.question: Joseph gathered all the money in Egypt and Canaan. Where did he bring it?
adults.option_a: He brought it into Pharaoh's house
adults.option_b: He kept it
adults.option_c: He distributed it to the poor
adults.explanation: Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh's house. See Genesis 47:14.

adults.question: What did Joseph do with the Egyptian people after buying their land?
adults.option_b: He sent them away
adults.option_c: He removed them to cities from one end of the borders of Egypt to the other
adults.explanation: Joseph removed the people to cities from one end of Egypt to the other. See Genesis 47:21.

adults.question: What oath did Joseph swear to Jacob regarding his burial?
adults.option_a: Jacob asked Joseph to put his hand under his thigh and swear not to bury him in Egypt
adults.option_b: A simple promise
adults.explanation: Jacob said "Put thy hand under my thigh" and swear. See Genesis 47:29-30.

adults.question: How did Israel respond after Joseph swore the oath about burial?
adults.option_b: He blessed Joseph
adults.option_d: Israel bowed himself upon the bed's head
adults.explanation: Israel bowed himself upon the bed's head. See Genesis 47:31.

adults.question: What does Joseph's economic policy during the famine reveal about his administrative approach?
adults.option_b: He centralised all resources under Pharaoh but provided seed for planting and kept the people alive, balancing survival with governance
adults.explanation: Joseph preserved life while consolidating resources under Pharaoh. See Genesis 47:23-26.

kids.question: Whose two sons did Jacob bless in this chapter?
kids.explanation: Jacob blessed Joseph's two sons Ephraim and Manasseh. See Genesis 48:1, 5.

kids.question: What unusual thing did Jacob do with his hands when blessing the boys?
kids.option_b: He crossed his hands and put his right hand on the younger son's head
kids.explanation: Israel stretched out his right hand and laid it upon Ephraim's head, who was the younger, crossing his hands. See Genesis 48:14.

kids.question: Which son received the greater blessing?
kids.explanation: Jacob set Ephraim before Manasseh, giving the younger the greater blessing. See Genesis 48:19-20.

teens.question: What did Jacob declare about Ephraim and Manasseh?
teens.option_d: They would be counted as his own sons, like Reuben and Simeon, receiving tribal inheritance
teens.explanation: Jacob adopted them saying "they are mine; as Reuben and Simeon, they shall be mine." See Genesis 48:5.

teens.question: Why did Joseph try to move Jacob's right hand to Manasseh's head?
teens.option_a: He thought Jacob made a mistake
teens.option_b: Joseph was displeased because Manasseh was the firstborn and should have the right hand
teens.explanation: Joseph tried to move Jacob's hand because Manasseh was the firstborn. See Genesis 48:17-18.

teens.question: What did Jacob say about why he put Ephraim first?
teens.option_c: God told him in a dream
teens.option_d: He knew what he was doing; Manasseh would be great but Ephraim would be greater and his seed would become a multitude of nations
teens.explanation: Jacob said Ephraim's seed shall become a multitude of nations. See Genesis 48:19.

teens.question: What did Jacob tell Joseph about Rachel?
teens.option_a: Rachel died by him in Canaan on the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem
teens.explanation: Jacob mentioned Rachel's death and burial near Bethlehem. See Genesis 48:7.

teens.question: What blessing formula did Jacob establish for Israel using these boys?
teens.option_b: May you be strong as lions
teens.option_c: In thee shall Israel bless, saying God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh
teens.explanation: Jacob said "In thee shall Israel bless, saying, God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh." See Genesis 48:20.

adults.question: What appearance of God did Jacob recall when blessing Ephraim and Manasseh?
adults.option_b: The angel at Peniel
adults.option_d: God Almighty appeared to him at Luz (Bethel) in Canaan and blessed him
adults.explanation: Jacob recalled God Almighty appearing to him at Luz. See Genesis 48:3.

adults.question: How did Jacob describe the angel who redeemed him?
adults.option_b: A ministering spirit
adults.option_c: The Angel which redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads
adults.explanation: Jacob invoked "The Angel which redeemed me from all evil." See Genesis 48:16.

adults.question: What did Jacob say his name and the names of Abraham and Isaac should be upon?
adults.option_b: Upon the lads Ephraim and Manasseh, and let them grow into a multitude
adults.explanation: Jacob said "let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers." See Genesis 48:16.

adults.question: What extra portion did Jacob give Joseph beyond his brothers?
adults.option_a: One portion above thy brethren which I took from the Amorite with my sword and bow
adults.option_b: Double the money
adults.explanation: Jacob gave Joseph one portion (Shechem) above his brethren. See Genesis 48:22.

adults.question: What is the Hebrew word for the portion Jacob gave Joseph, and what city does it refer to?
adults.option_b: Hebron
adults.option_c: Shechem, meaning both "shoulder/portion" and the city of Shechem
adults.explanation: The Hebrew word shechem means shoulder or portion, and refers to the city. See Genesis 48:22.

adults.question: How does Jacob's crossing of hands continue a pattern seen throughout Genesis?
adults.option_a: It continues the pattern of the younger being chosen over the elder: Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, now Ephraim over Manasseh
adults.option_b: It was accidental
adults.explanation: God consistently chose the younger over the elder throughout Genesis. See Genesis 48:14, 19.

adults.question: What did Jacob acknowledge about his own failing eyesight?
adults.option_b: He had been healed
adults.option_d: His eyes were dim for age so that he could not see clearly, paralleling his father Isaac
adults.explanation: Israel's eyes were dim for age. See Genesis 48:10.

adults.question: What prophetic statement did Jacob make about Israel's future relationship with Canaan?
adults.option_a: They would stay in Egypt forever
adults.option_b: God shall be with you and bring you again unto the land of your fathers
adults.explanation: Jacob prophesied God would bring them back to Canaan. See Genesis 48:21.

kids.question: What did Jacob do before he died?
kids.option_a: He gave a special blessing or prophecy to each of his twelve sons
kids.explanation: Jacob called his sons and blessed them, every one with his own blessing. See Genesis 49:1, 28.

kids.question: What animal did Jacob compare Judah to?
kids.explanation: Jacob said "Judah is a lion's whelp." See Genesis 49:9.

kids.question: Where did Jacob ask to be buried?
kids.option_c: In the cave of Machpelah where Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, and Rebekah were buried
kids.explanation: Jacob asked to be buried in the cave of Machpelah. See Genesis 49:29-30.

teens.question: Why did Reuben lose his place of honour as firstborn?
teens.option_d: He was unstable as water and had defiled his father's bed by lying with Bilhah
teens.explanation: Reuben defiled his father's couch and was called unstable as water. See Genesis 49:3-4.

teens.question: What did Jacob prophesy about Judah and the sceptre?
teens.option_b: The sceptre shall not depart from Judah nor a lawgiver from between his feet until Shiloh come
teens.explanation: Jacob prophesied the sceptre would remain with Judah until Shiloh come. See Genesis 49:10.

teens.question: What animal did Jacob compare Dan to?
teens.option_d: A serpent by the way, an adder in the path
teens.explanation: Jacob said "Dan shall be a serpent by the way, an adder in the path." See Genesis 49:17.

teens.question: What did Jacob say about Joseph?
teens.option_a: Joseph is a fruitful bough by a well whose branches run over the wall
teens.option_d: Joseph would return to Canaan alone
teens.explanation: Jacob described Joseph as a fruitful bough by a well. See Genesis 49:22.

teens.question: What animal did Jacob compare Benjamin to?
teens.option_c: A wolf that raveneth, dividing the spoil
teens.explanation: Jacob said "Benjamin shall ravin as a wolf." See Genesis 49:27.

adults.question: What does the Shiloh prophecy in Genesis 49:10 traditionally signify?
adults.option_b: A peace treaty
adults.option_d: A Messianic prophecy that the ruler would come from Judah's line, understood as a reference to Christ
adults.explanation: Shiloh is widely interpreted as a Messianic title meaning "he to whom it belongs." See Genesis 49:10.

adults.question: What prophecy did Jacob give about Simeon and Levi together?
adults.option_a: They would be blessed with land
adults.option_b: They would be priests together
adults.option_c: He cursed their anger and said he would divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel
adults.explanation: Jacob cursed their fierce anger and said they would be scattered. See Genesis 49:5-7.

adults.question: What did Jacob say about Zebulun's future location?
adults.option_b: He shall dwell at the haven of the sea and be for an haven of ships, and his border unto Zidon
adults.explanation: Zebulun would dwell by the sea as a haven of ships. See Genesis 49:13.

adults.question: How did Jacob describe Issachar?
adults.option_a: A strong ass couching down between two burdens
adults.option_b: A mighty warrior
adults.explanation: Issachar is a strong ass couching down between two burdens. See Genesis 49:14.

adults.question: What did Jacob say about Naphtali?
adults.option_b: He would conquer many nations
adults.option_c: He is a hind (doe) let loose who giveth goodly words
adults.explanation: Naphtali is a hind let loose, he giveth goodly words. See Genesis 49:21.

adults.question: What specific blessings did Jacob pronounce upon Joseph?
adults.option_a: Blessings of heaven above, blessings of the deep, blessings of the breasts and of the womb, prevailing unto the utmost bound of the everlasting hills
adults.option_b: Wealth only
adults.explanation: Jacob gave Joseph blessings of heaven, deep, breasts, and womb. See Genesis 49:25-26.

adults.question: Who are specifically named as buried in the cave of Machpelah?
adults.option_b: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob only
adults.option_d: Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, and Leah
adults.explanation: Abraham, Sarah, Isaac, Rebekah, and Leah are named as buried there. See Genesis 49:31.

adults.question: What did Jacob do immediately after finishing his blessings and burial instructions?
adults.option_b: He gathered up his feet into the bed, yielded up the ghost, and was gathered unto his people
adults.explanation: Jacob gathered his feet into the bed, yielded up the ghost, and was gathered unto his people. See Genesis 49:33.

kids.question: Where was Jacob buried?
kids.option_a: In the cave of Machpelah in Canaan
kids.explanation: They buried Jacob in the cave of Machpelah. See Genesis 50:13.

kids.question: What kind words did Joseph say to his brothers after their father died?
kids.option_b: You meant it for evil but God meant it for good to save many people
kids.explanation: Joseph said "ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good." See Genesis 50:20.

kids.question: How old was Joseph when he died?
kids.option_b: One hundred and twenty years old
kids.option_c: A hundred and ten years old
kids.explanation: Joseph died being an hundred and ten years old. See Genesis 50:26.

teens.question: What did the Egyptians do when Jacob died?
teens.option_d: They mourned for him seventy days and embalmed him
teens.explanation: The Egyptians mourned Jacob seventy days, and the physicians embalmed him. See Genesis 50:2-3.

teens.question: What were the brothers afraid of after Jacob's death?
teens.option_b: That Joseph would now take revenge on them for selling him
teens.explanation: The brothers feared Joseph would hate them and requite their evil. See Genesis 50:15.

teens.question: What message did the brothers claim Jacob had left for Joseph?
teens.option_d: Forgive the trespass of thy brethren and their sin, for they did unto thee evil
teens.explanation: They said Jacob commanded Joseph to forgive them. See Genesis 50:16-17.

teens.question: What did Joseph do when his brothers fell down before him?
teens.option_a: He wept and said fear not, for am I in the place of God
teens.explanation: Joseph wept and told them not to fear. See Genesis 50:17-19.

teens.question: What promise did Joseph make his brothers swear about his bones?
teens.option_a: To build him a great tomb
teens.option_b: To bury him by the Nile
teens.option_c: To carry his bones out of Egypt when God visited them and brought them to the promised land
teens.option_d: To place him in Pharaoh's tomb
teens.explanation: Joseph made them swear to carry his bones out of Egypt. See Genesis 50:25.

adults.question: How long did the mourning for Jacob last in total, including the Egyptian and Canaanite periods?
adults.option_b: Thirty days
adults.option_d: Seventy days of Egyptian mourning plus seven days at the threshing floor of Atad
adults.explanation: Seventy days in Egypt and seven more at the threshing floor of Atad. See Genesis 50:3-4, 10.

adults.question: What did the Canaanites call the place of mourning when they saw the grieving?
adults.option_b: Machpelah
adults.option_c: Abel-mizraim, meaning the mourning of the Egyptians
adults.explanation: They called it Abel-mizraim (mourning of Egypt). See Genesis 50:11.

adults.question: What is the full statement of Joseph about God's sovereignty in Genesis 50:20?
adults.option_b: Ye thought evil against me but God meant it unto good, to bring to pass as it is this day, to save much people alive
adults.explanation: Joseph declared God turned their evil intent into good to save many lives. See Genesis 50:20.

adults.question: What did Joseph say about his role in relation to God when his brothers were afraid?
adults.option_a: Am I in the place of God? Fear not, I will nourish you and your little ones
adults.option_b: I am your ruler
adults.explanation: Joseph humbly said "Am I in the place of God?" See Genesis 50:19-21.

adults.question: What did Joseph prophesy to his brothers before he died?
adults.option_b: They would become Egyptians
adults.option_c: God will surely visit you and bring you out of this land unto the land which he sware to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
adults.explanation: Joseph said "God will surely visit you, and bring you out of this land." See Genesis 50:24.

adults.question: What was done with Joseph's body after death?
adults.option_a: He was embalmed and put in a coffin in Egypt
adults.option_b: He was buried immediately
adults.explanation: They embalmed Joseph and he was put in a coffin in Egypt. See Genesis 50:26.

adults.question: How does Genesis 50:20 serve as a theological summary of the entire Joseph narrative?
adults.option_b: It only applies to Joseph
adults.option_d: It demonstrates that God sovereignly works through human evil to accomplish His redemptive purposes, a theme running from the brothers' betrayal through famine relief
adults.explanation: Genesis 50:20 encapsulates divine sovereignty over human sin for redemptive ends.

adults.question: Joseph's instruction about his bones was fulfilled centuries later. When and by whom?
adults.option_a: By David when he became king
adults.option_b: By Moses during the Exodus, as recorded in Exodus 13:19, and Joseph was finally buried at Shechem per Joshua 24:32
adults.explanation: Moses took Joseph's bones at the Exodus and they were buried at Shechem. See Exodus 13:19 and Joshua 24:32.
